To ask the Minister of Education whether any flexibility exists to transfer unallocated pre-school pupil places from undersubscribed provisions to those that are oversubscribed in the same local area.
answer_text: It is a matter for the Education Authority (EA) to secure sufficient pre-school education provision in local areas to meet overall demand. Funded places for non-statutory pre-school settings are allocated by the EA on an annual basis based on an assessment of need. Places in statutory pre-school settings are approved via the statutory Development Proposal process and are  funded under the  Common Funding Scheme. The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  A ‘transfer’ of places between settings is therefore not possible.  Unfilled pre-school places are not funded by my Department or the EA.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered the introduction of a clearly defined and operational category for autistic individuals with the highest and most complex support needs, as proposed by experts commissioned by The Lancet, in order to address identified gaps in service access, commissioning, and pathway clarity within existing Programmes of Care.
answer_text: My Department is aware that the Lancet Commission on the future of care and clinical research in autism has previously proposed that consideration be made around adopting the designation of profound autism as an administrative term to apply to children and adults with the most complex support needs. The Commission suggested this designation for people who have very limited or no language, significant intellectual disability (typically an IQ below 50), and a requirement for 24-hour supervision, with the intention of improving service planning and ensuring this group is not overlooked in research or commissioning.The Department is also aware that this proposed label is not universally agreed within the research or autistic communities. Several academics and autistic scholars have raised substantial concerns about the validity and usefulness of this classification. For example, Kapp (2023) argues that the term “profound autism” lacks a clear evidence-based definition, relies on problematic functioning labels, and risks over-simplifying the diverse strengths and needs of autistic individuals. Given these differing perspectives, my Department recognises that while the Lancet proposal aims to support service planning, any movement toward adopting an additional operational category would require careful consideration. This includes engagement with clinicians across Programmes of Care, researchers, autistic people and families, and alignment with regional strategies including the Autism Strategy 2023-2028.At present, my Department is continuing to monitor emerging evidence and debate in this area but has not adopted a separate diagnostic or operational category beyond existing frameworks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) monitoring (ii) enforcement; and (iii) audit mechanisms, in place to ensure compliance with accessibility standards for pavement use.
answer_text: My Department’s routine maintenance inspections can identify obstructions that may cause accessibility issues to footway users.  Any obstruction recorded as a defect is addressed in accordance with my Departments Limited Service maintenance policy. Obstructions on footways can also be raised by members of the public or elected representatives via contact with local section offices or by using NIDirect.The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 states that any person who, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, in any way intentionally or negligently obstructs the free passage along a road shall be guilty of an offence. A "road" includes footways.Under Article 87 of the Order, my officals can remove obstructions such as signs or advertisements and seek to recover any expenses thereby reasonably incurred.The location of new street furniture is subject to the guidance contained in the Department for Transport Inclusive Mobility, A Guide to Best Practice on Access to Pedestrian and Transport Infrastructure.The siting of temporary signage related to road works is controlled by the Safety at Street Works and Road Works Code of Practice and Chapter 8 of the Traffic Signs.  Works promoters are required to design and implement temporary traffic management that maintains safe and suitable access for all users, with particular regard to the disabled, elderly, and those with visual or mobility impairments. My Department ensures compliance with the relevant codes and accessibility requirements through permitting and licensing arrangements, routine inspections by my officials, and enforcement where necessary.Additionally, in planning and delivering new infrastructure, officials draw on policy documents that reference the Department for Transport’s Inclusive Mobility guidance and maintain regular engagement with Section 75 representative groups, including IMTAC, to ensure that the needs of vulnerable pedestrians are properly considered.The first phase of the “Including People” strand of my “Foundations for a Better Future” initiative saw older, Deaf, and disabled people raise concerns around maintenance of pavements and the particular impact of poorly maintained pavements on those who use wheelchairs or who have reduced mobility. As the “Including People” work strand enters a second phase, my officials will continue to consider how best to address the issues which have been raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the default option for routine postnatal contacts in the first 10 days is home visiting, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Across all Health and Social Care Trusts, the default option for routine postnatal contacts within the first 10 days following birth is home visiting. While clinicbased or telephone contacts may be offered where clinically appropriate or at the request of the mother, home visits remain the standard and preferred model of postnatal care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of postnatal contacts for new mothers and their babies that were (i) clinic-based; and (ii) home visit-based, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) have advised that they are unable to provide a breakdown of postnatal contacts by clinicbased and homevisitbased activity, as their systems do not record contacts in this way and providing such detail would require an unfeasible manual trawl of individual records.The Northern HSCT has recently moved to electronic recording of postnatal contacts and is therefore able to provide indicative data from 2025 onwards. This shows that approximately 57% of contacts in that year were home visits and 42% were delivered in clinic settings. The Belfast, South Eastern and Western HSCTs have each confirmed that postnatal contacts are delivered primarily through home visits.The default approach in all Trusts for routine postnatal contacts within the first 10 days following birth is a home visit. While clinicbased or telephone contacts may be provided where clinically appropriate or at the request of the mother, home visiting remains the standard and preferred model of postnatal care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any plans she has to introduce targeted support schemes before the end of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate in order to assist town centre businesses facing ongoing cost pressures and help prevent further high street closures.
answer_text: Supporting our town centres is a complex, crosscutting issue which cannot be addressed by a single department or through isolated interventions. Responsibility rests primarily with the Department for Communities, working in partnership with local councils, while responsibility for rating and rates relief lies with the Department of Finance.  The Finance Minister has recently concluded a consultation on Small Business Rate Relief and is currently assessing the findings from that process with a view to bringing proposals to the Executive.  He has also stated that there is £10m set aside in his draft Multi-Year Budget to provide support for businesses in the context of that consultation.However, my Department does provide a range of support for SMEs through Intertrade Ireland and Invest NI who offer a broad mix of export, innovation, capability building, and funding supports designed to help businesses grow sales, modernise operations, develop new products, and expand into new marketsI have also committed £45 million through the Regional Balance Fund to empower Local Economic Partnerships to address specific issues in their areas and drive productivity. I remain committed to working collaboratively with Executive colleagues and councils to ensure coordinated and effective support for town centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of recognising Covid-19 as an industrial disease, for the purposes of Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit.
answer_text: The Industrial Injuries Advisory Council (IIAC), an independent scientific body, advises my Department and the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) on changes to the list of prescribed diseases for which Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit can be paid. IIAC has published 2 Command Papers on COVID-19 and its occupational impacts. The DWP is currently carrying out a detailed assessment of the recommendations in these papers, and my officials will continue to liaise with the DWP on this work including any proposals for future legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of schools included in the third tranche of part-time 20mph speed limit zones, which are located within the North Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3. This Tranche has predominantly focused on schools in rural areas where the national speed limit would normally apply.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits which is open for consultation. Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the review of Historical Institutional Abuse services, as outlined in their Department's Business Plan 2025-2026.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any assessment his Department has made of the impact of transitional protection on Universal Credit claimants in Northern Ireland, following the removal of the two-child limit.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any further legislation, including any commencement or operational regulations, required to bring into force the provisions of the Child Support Enforcement Act (Northern Ireland) 2025 relating to Administrative Liability Orders; (ii) the current status of that work; and (iii) a timetable for when those provisions are expected to become operational.
answer_text: A Statutory Rule and a Commencement Order will be required for the Child Maintenance Service to bring into operation the Administrative Liability Orders introduced by the Child Support Enforcement Act (Northern Ireland) 2025. My Department will maintain parity with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) when bringing forward the necessary legislation. The DWP are working with His Majesty’s Courts and Tribunals  Service and the Scottish Government to establish a process for implementing Administrative Liability Orders and plan to introduce regulations to Parliament as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any currently live (a) policies; (b) strategies; (c) programmes; or (d) action plans, within his Department that have an explicit objective of tackling poverty; and (ii) the stated aim of each policy or programme.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for leading the Executive’s work to tackle poverty. It is however currently taking forward a number of initiatives to tackle educational disadvantage and ensure that all children can access a full educational experience. These initiatives include free school meals, uniform grants, the Extended Schools programme, the RAISE programme, the expansion of full-time pre-school education to all pupils and the childcare subsidy scheme.The provision of free school meals offers a nutritious school lunch to over 90,000 children and young people on each school day. This continues the longstanding approach of providing FSM to those in low-income families that meet the eligibility criteria. This helps to address educational disadvantage through targeted support to families on low incomes to ensure that those children most in need have access to a meal which is suitable as their main meal of the day and therefore are in the best position to access their education.Similarly, uniform grants are also available to assist families on low incomes to purchase of school uniform items in order that their children are able to access a full educational experience.  In addition, I have introduced the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill which will shortly become law.  This will address affordability of school uniforms for parents, as well as comfort and practicality for pupils. The Pre-School Standardisation Programme intends, over time, to standardise pre-school education session lengths for all target aged children to 22.5 hours per week, with access to a free school meal for eligible children.In recognition that childcare costs can be a driver of financial stress and poverty risk among working families in Northern Ireland, the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) currently provides a 15% cost reduction for childcare, and is available to families eligible for TaxFree Childcare (TFC).  Over time and subject to Executive funding, I intend to increase the NICSS contribution to 40%, and when combined with TFC, this would reduce childcare bills for eligible families by over 50%.  The Extended Schools programme has been fully operational since 2006 and aims to improve levels of educational achievement for disadvantaged children and young people by providing the additional support that they might need to help them reach their full potential. Since 2006, my Department has invested over £180m of Extended Schools funding in order to ensure those schools serving the most disadvantaged areas can offer a wide range of additional services and activities outside the normal school day to help meet the learning and development needs of pupils, their families and local communities. The RAISE Programme is a whole community, place-based approach to tackling educational disadvantage in 18 localities across Northern Ireland.  The strategic objectives for the programme are raising achievement and addressing educational disadvantage; and

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail all currently live policies, strategies, programmes and action plans that have an explicit objective of tackling poverty, including (i) the lead Department; and (ii) the policy or programme’s stated aim.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on all currently live initiatives across the Executive Departments. These strategies, programmes and action plans are the responsibility of the relevant individual Departments.Once it has been agreed by the Executive, it is intended that the Anti-Poverty Strategy, which my Department is leading on, will provide a framework for departments’ future work to address poverty across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how gender-disaggregated and intersectional data is used to inform (i) funding decisions; (ii) monitoring; and (iii) evaluation, under the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls strategy.
answer_text: We are continuing to build an EVAWG evidence base by drawing on official statistics and developing OutcomesBased Accountability (OBA) reporting for EVAWG Delivery Plan implementation, in order to strengthen our understanding of women’s and girls’ experiences and intersectional implications.Projectlevel information from the EVAWG Regional and Local Change Fund helps track reach and impact, identify gaps and support funding decisions and policy development.We are continuing to work closely with NISRA statisticians who publish key genderdisaggregated datasets, and with wider delivery partners, including the PSNI, to address recognised data gaps and to develop a more joined up, insight -driven approach. Additionally, our officials are liaising with officials through the Five Jurisdictions Working Group to share information and learn from best practice in addressing common data challenges in neighbouring jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he will (i) review; or (ii) amend, the rule within the Discretionary Support Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2016 that, save in the case of a disaster, only one Discretionary Support grant may be made within any rolling 12-month period, in light of case examples in the Office of the Discretionary Support Commissioner's Annual Report 2024-2025, where this rule restricted support for households facing serious hardship, despite verified need.
answer_text: Discretionary Support claimants may be considered for up to two grants in a 12 month rolling period; one grant for items and one grant for living expenses, subject to eligibility criteria.  In addition, further grant awards may be made where there is evidence of a disaster. Proposed amendments to the Discretionary Support Scheme are currently under consideration.  Any changes will need to be assessed within the context of ongoing financial constraint, with a focus to ensuring that the Scheme remains financially sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will review how savings are treated within Discretionary Support eligibility, including in cases where savings are set aside for funeral costs, given there is no disregard provided for in the current regulations, and the example highlighted in the Office of the Discretionary Support Commissioner Annual Report 2024-2025 where a claim was refused despite no previous Discretionary Support awards.
answer_text: The Discretionary Support Scheme is designed to provide financial assistance in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.  Disregards are applied to protect basic essentials, such as housing costs.  Where a person has accessible resources, these are expected to be used to address the immediate crisis need, in full or in part, before a Discretionary Support award can be considered. Amendments to the Discretionary Support Scheme are under consideration.  Any changes will need to be assessed within the context of ongoing financial constraint, with a focus to ensuring that the Scheme remains financially sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reason the success rate for first reviews carried out by Review Officers fell from 27 per cent in 2023-2024 to 15 per cent in 2024-2025, as reported the Office of the Discretionary Support Commissioner Annual Report 2024-2025 (covering 1 April 2024 to 31 March 2025); and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure (a) initial Discretionary Support decisions by Finance Support Officers; and (b) first review decisions, are accurate, consistent and evidence-based.
answer_text: The success rate of first reviews conducted by Discretionary Support Review Officers reduced in 2024/25 from 27% to 15% due to increased levels of accuracy in respect of the initial decisions being made by my Department’s Finance Support Officers. Accuracy of decision making in determining Discretionary Support awards remains a key priority for the branch, with a range of checks in place to ensure that decisions are made within the relevant guidance and legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department takes to monitor and engage with emerging international research on the impact of screen use and smartphone exposure on early childhood development.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a road safety assessment has been carried out by her Department, in relation to the closure of Fairhill Street, Ballycastle.
answer_text: The current vehicular closure of Fairhill Street by Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council is due to ongoing stability issues with the Antrim Arms Building and the need to prioritise pedestrians by implementing a safety zone.  Pedestrian access is being maintained along the road.The temporary traffic management arrangement at the Diamond is operating successfully with no reported incidents since its introduction.

answer_text: The current vehicular closure of Fairhill Street by Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council is due to ongoing stability issues with the Antrim Arms Building and the need to prioritise pedestrians by implementing a safety zone. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how his Department collates and uses data to inform legislative change.
To ask the Minister of Education, further to AQW 32546/22-27, to detail the names of all schools currently participating in the Extended Schools Programme, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The names of all schools participating in the Extended Schools Programme during the 2025/26 financial year, broken down by constituency has been deposited in the Assembly Library. Other information on eligible schools is routinely published on my Department’s website and is available at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/extended-schools-programme. The information placed in the Assembly Library also includes eligibility information pertaining to each school. Schools that are fully eligible receive their full funding allocation, schools assessed as buffering receive 50% of their core allocation and schools assessed as exiting or closing receive 25% of their core allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department works in partnership with specialist organisations, including women’s organisations and domestic abuse services, to design or deliver prevention work within sporting settings.
answer_text: Sport NI, as one of my Department’s Arms Length Bodies does undertake such engagement and has advised me as follows.“Sport NI engaged Women’s Aid Federation Northern Ireland to deliver Domestic Abuse in Sport Awareness Sessions (including Safe Leave Act) to governing bodies of sport November 2025.  36 participants across 26 Governing bodies attended the two sessions and completed evaluations stating their appreciation for the course, providing knowledge and guidance and welcomed any future training in this area.   Same session was offered to Sport NI staff and Line Managers including the Safe Leave Act requirements as a result of pending legislation in 2026”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) programmes; (ii) funding streams; or (iii) initiatives, supported by his Department, that engage sports clubs in addressing (a) domestic abuse; (b) misogyny; or (c) violence against women and girls.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Sport NI who have  advised me as follows-“Following requests for training in this area, Sport NI offered Keeping Adults Safe in Sport training to governing bodies in January and March 2025, which was facilitated by Volunteer Now.Sport NI then offered governing bodies Domestic Abuse in Sport Awareness Sessions (including Safe Leave Act) in November 2025, which was facilitated by Women’s Aid Federation NI. 36 participants across 26 Governing bodies attended the two sessions. Feedback welcomed the guidance provided and indicated they would be interested in future training in this area.Sport NI are currently working to procure an adult safeguarding service for the sports sector. This is scheduled to commence by the end of February 2026 and operate until March 2027. This will provide guidance, advice and support to the sports sector, including sports clubs, regarding adult safeguarding queries including violence against women and girls.Sport NI representatives are also scheduled to meet with TEO Local Change fund representatives to explore work through the Change Funding pot for work with men and boys in the sports sector. The meeting is scheduled for 3rd February 2026 and will look at the GAA Gamechanger Programme and IFA Fair Game project.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women on gynaecology waiting lists within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by (i) outpatient and inpatient waiting lists; and (ii) length of wait, including those waiting longer than (a) 13 weeks; (b) 26 weeks; (c) 52 weeks; and (d) 104 weeks.
answer_text: The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment and the number of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the gynaecology specialty are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin and the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient and Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin, respectively. Figures broken down by HSC Trust and time band are published in the accompanying data files. The most recent issues of these bulletins have a reporting date of 30 September 2025 and are available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-timeshttps://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-timesThis information has been provided by IAD.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any role his Department has, in contributing to the ending of violence against women and girls, including work with men and boys through community and grassroots sports clubs.
answer_text: As part of the development of the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy, my Department made significant commitments. These include ensuring quality frontline services and protection for victims and survivors of violence against women and girls, alongside several other initiatives through the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. In relation to sports, following requests for training in this area, Sport NI offered Keeping Adults Safe in Sport training to governing bodies in January and March 2025, which was facilitated by Volunteer Now.Sport NI also offered governing bodies Domestic Abuse in Sport Awareness Sessions which were facilitated in November 2025 by Women’s Aid Federation. 36 participants across 26 Governing bodies attended the two sessions. Sport NI sit as a representative body on Safeguarding in Sport Strategic Group where EVAWG strategy forms part of the discussions around the work the group does. Sport NI are currently working to procure an adult safeguarding service for the sports sector. This is scheduled to commence by the end of February 2026 and operate until March 2027. Sport NI representatives are also scheduled to meet with TEO Local Change fund representatives to explore work through the Change Funding pot for work with men and boys in the sports sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment carried out on the potential impact on pupils who will remain based on the Dunluce School site, Bushmills, once the three site amalgamation of Causeway Academy begins in September 2026.
answer_text: Causeway Academy will operate across three campuses when it opens in 2026 to ensure a smooth and supportive transition. Key Stage 3 students (Years 8-10) will begin their journey at the Carthall Road Campus (previously Coleraine College) and a new Sixth Form (Year 13) will be launched at the Cloyfin Campus (previously North Coast Integrated College). Students in years 11, 12 and 14, including those at Dunluce School, will remain on the campus of their legacy school to complete their studies and examinations. School assemblies were held with students across all three legacy campuses, including Dunluce School, this has ensured that students are fully informed about the phased transition. In addition, a structured ‘student voice’ exercise has been completed, enabling students from Dunluce School to share views on the supports, activities and information they feel they need during the transition period.Informed by this engagement, the transition plan for pupils who will remain at the Bushmills Campus (Dunluce School) includes:Continuity of teaching and curriculum provision for pupils completing Key Stage 4.Onsite pastoral support, including Pastoral staffA programme of Causeway Academy transition activities, to build familiarity and confidence.Continued engagement and communication of what the remainder of this school year will look like. This assessment process will continue throughout 2026 to ensure that pupils at the Bushmills Campus (Dunluce School) are supported academically, socially, and emotionally during the transition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Carer’s Allowance overpayment cases that have resulted in (i) deductions from ongoing benefit; (ii) referral to his Department’s Debt Management Unit; and (iii) legal proceedings, in each year between 2015 and 2025.
answer_text: (i) The information is not available in the format requested. Overpayments which have been fully repaid are archived and deleted from our systems, in line with GDPR and the Department’s Retention and Disposal Schedule. However, figures show that during December 2025, 1,990 individuals had a deduction from their benefit* to repay a Carer’s Allowance overpayment. *this deduction could be from Carer’s Allowance or another benefit in payment(ii) The table below shows the number of Carer’s Allowance overpayment cases referred to the Department’s Debt Management Unit for recovery in each year between 2015 and 2025.Recoverable Carer’s Allowance Overpayment cases referred to Debt Management *Financial YearVolumes2015/161,9592016/171,0632017/189872018/191,0492019/201,6392020/219122021/222,2782022/232,1692023/242,0672024/252,5232025/26**2,945*The figures show the number of individual Carer’s Allowance overpayments referred during the relevant period. An individual may have more than one Carer’s Allowance overpayment. The figures do not include non-recoverable overpayments.**Apr - Dec 2025 inclusive(iii) The information is not available in the format requested. Cases which have been fully repaid, including those subject to legal proceedings, are archived and deleted in line with GDPR and the Department’s Retention and Disposal Schedule. However, figures on 31st December 2025, show that 133 individuals have been subject to legal proceedings* to recover a Carer’s Allowance overpayment. An additional 236 individuals with a Carer’s Allowance overpayment have been subject to legal proceedings* in relation to benefit fraud. *in the majority of these cases legal proceedings will have been initiated prior to December 2025.The data provided in all answers above is taken from operational administrative systems and is not intended for publication. Therefore, the data itself is not quality assured to the standard of published Official Statistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department had with pupils of Dunluce School, Bushmills, before the decision was made to establish Causeway Academy over three campuses.
answer_text: Once a Development Proposal (DP) is approved it becomes a statutory obligation for the proposer, in this case the Education Authority (EA), to proceed with its implementation. The decision of Causeway Academy to operate over three campuses from September 2026 was discussed at length by the Interim Board of Governors working with the Principal Designate and EA.  In taking forward the operation of Causeway Academy on the Bushmills, Carthall and Cloyfin Campuses, educational and pastoral considerations were considered.  School assemblies were held with students across all three legacy campuses, including Dunluce School, to ensure that students are fully informed about the phased transition. In addition, a structured ‘student voice’ exercise has been completed, enabling students from Dunluce School to share views on the supports, activities and information they feel they need during the transition period.Key findings from this engagement include:Strong pupil identity, with students expressing both pride in their current school environment and curiosity about Causeway Academy.The importance of continuity, particularly for pupils entering Key Stage 4 in September 2026 who will complete their studies on-site.A desire for enhanced transition activities, including opportunities to meet future Causeway Academy peers, explore the Carthall Campus, and understand new routines and expectations.The need for ongoing communication, through regular assemblies, updates to families, and opportunities for pupils to raise concerns as the amalgamation progresses.Informed by this engagement, a transition plan for pupils who will remain at the Bushmills Campus (Dunluce School) was developed. Updates for Causeway Academy, its mission and vision are available on the school’s website and prospectus:  www.causeway-academy.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail which recommendations from the Sayce Review of Carer’s Allowance Overpayments his Department considers applicable to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials continue to work closely with Department for Work and Pensions counterparts and with local stakeholders to discuss the recommendations from the Independent Review of CA Overpayments. This is an ongoing piece of work, however, it is standard practice to maintain parity with operational and policy changes relating to the social security system in Northern Ireland.

answer_text: My officials are considering the replies to the consultation on the draft Local Government (Cremation) Regulations (NI) 2024 and in context of the provisions within the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill which passed its Final Assembly Stage in December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the hospitals (i) with a Changing Places toilet facility, including where the facility is located within the hospital; and (ii) which do not currently have a Changing Places toilet facility.
answer_text: Below are the details of the number and location of Changing Places facilities provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.    Northern Health and Social Care Trust  The NHSCT has two Changing Places facilities with one located at Antrim Hospital and one within the Ballymena Health & Care Centre.     South Eastern Health and Care Trust  The SEHSCT has one Changing Places facility within the Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre located on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site.  Southern Health and Social Care Trust  The SHSCT has two fully compliant Changing Places facilities with one located at the Banbridge CTCC site and one located at the Blossom Paediatrics building at Craigavon Area Hospital.     Belfast Health and Social Care Trust  The BHSCT does not yet have a Changing Place facility in any of its acute hospital sites.     Western Health and Social Care Trust   No response received.  Those facilities not listed do not currently have a register Changing Places facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the UK Government’s announcement that the two-child limit in Universal Credit and Child Tax Credit will be abolished, to detail (i) any specific actions he intends to take in the next 30 days; and (ii) any further measures he will put in place before the end of this financial year, to alleviate poverty in Northern Ireland, prior to that change taking effect.
answer_text: My officials will continue to work to ensure that these changes can be delivered in Northern Ireland at the same time as the rest of the UK. I would encourage any families that are struggling, or in crisis, to contact the ‘Make the Call’ team to ensure they are accessing all the benefits they are entitled to. This may include the Universal Credit New Claims Grant or Discretionary Support.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any actions their Department is taking to ensure rural and semi-rural communities in North Antrim, which are impacted by paramilitarism and drugs, benefit from equivalent investment and support.

To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the UK Government’s decision to abolish the two-child limit in Universal Credit and Child Tax Credit, to detail any impact this change will have on the future (i) design; and (ii) funding requirements, of the welfare mitigation package.
answer_text: There are no plans to change the design of the welfare mitigation package.  My officials are currently working to assess the full impact of the abolition of the two-child limit, which will include any additional funding requirements for welfare mitigations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the UK Government’s decision to abolish the two-child limit in Universal Credit and Child Tax Credit, to detail (i) the projected additional cost to the existing benefit cap mitigation arising from potential increases in entitlement for larger families; (ii) whether he will ensure that this additional cost is fully funded within the welfare mitigation package; and (iii) whether he will ensure that the Discretionary Support Scheme will not be reduced as a result of these changes.
answer_text: I welcome the UK Government’s decision to abolish the two-child limit, as this will help alleviate the hardship faced by many families and contribute to reducing child poverty.  		Due to data complexities, the projected additional cost to the existing benefit cap mitigation arising from potential increases in entitlement for larger families is not yet available.  My officials are working to quantify the full financial implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timetable for (i) publication; and (ii) implementation, of the Children and Young People’s Strategy Delivery Plan 2024–2028, in line with the duties under the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (NI) 2015.
answer_text: A refreshed Children and Young People’s Strategy Delivery Plan for 2025 – 2028 is currently being finalised and will be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any analysis undertaken of in-cash and in-kind child-contingent benefits in ROI, including whether similar supports could be modelled for NI.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any analysis of in-cash and in-kind child-contingent benefits in the ROI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions being taken to reduce stigma associated with claiming social security benefits.
answer_text: My Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service (MtC) aims to ensure that claimants receive all the benefits, supports, and services to which they are entitled. The Service provides free and confidential needs assessments to identify eligibility for social security benefits and additional supports and/or services.Staff within MtC are committed to engaging with empathy and respect and  processed 65,751 telephone calls and 12,156 home visits in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a timeline for the closure of the Ross Thomson Unit in the Causeway Hospital has been proposed.
answer_text: A timeline for the closure of the Ross Thomson Unit in the Causeway Hospital has not been proposed. This would be dependent upon creating alternative facilities, as planned.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the availability of Changing Places facilities in Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any estimate his Department has made of the total value of unclaimed social security benefits; and (ii) any steps being taken to increase uptake.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.My Department takes a number of steps to ensure individuals and families access their full benefit entitlements. The Make the Call Wraparound Service (MtC) provides free, confidential needs assessments to identify eligibility for social security benefits and additional supports. Through partnerships with community organisations and government departments, MtC Outreach Officers raise awareness and offer home visits for vulnerable individuals. A comprehensive communications strategy across television, press, and digital channels supports these efforts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered introducing and routinely publishing poverty indicators for Northern Ireland, as in the Republic of Ireland, which measure at-risk-of-poverty, enforced deprivation and consistent poverty.
answer_text: The official poverty measures for Northern Ireland (NI) and across the UK are relative and absolute poverty, which can be presented on both a before and after housing costs basis. The NI Poverty and Income Inequality Report is the primary source for statistics about poverty and income inequality in NI. Using data from the Family Resources Survey (FRS), it provides official annual estimates of the proportion of people, children, working-age adults and pensioners in NI living in poverty, and other statistics on material deprivation and income inequality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any assessment his Department has made of the economic cost of child poverty in Northern Ireland as a proportion of GDP, similar to the Economic and Social Research Institute's estimate of 3.9 per cent in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Responsibility for such an assessment would rest with the Department for Communities, given its policy responsibilities in relation to anti-poverty, and its lead role in developing the Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has reviewed the qualitative evaluation of the Scottish Child Payment.
answer_text: My Department has not reviewed the qualitative evaluation of the Scottish Child Payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Joseph Rowntree Foundation and Trussell's Guarantee Our Essentials campaign, which calls for a guaranteed minimum income within Universal Credit.
answer_text: I have not carried out an assessment of this campaign.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of the North South Interconnector.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of the Ballycastle Shared Education Campus project.
answer_text: Following the success of Limavady Shared Education Campus, which opened in 2023, I am delighted to be able to report that the Ballycastle Shared Campus is making excellent progress in construction.This project will deliver Northern Ireland’s first purpose-built shared education campus, with an investment of £70m from my Department through the Fresh Start Programme.Ballycastle High School and Cross and Passion College have a strong sense of history, with strong sharing arrangements that have developed and strengthened over many years.Both schools have benefited from the loyalty demonstrated by the local community and it is wonderful that my Department is able to return that continued support in such a tangible way, with the new Shared Education Campus.Construction of the main school building is due to complete next summer and I expect the pupils from both schools to be able to start next academic year in their new shared building.  The remaining works are programmed to complete before the end of 2027 and will include the provision of sports pitches, a sports hall with facilities for EA’s Youth Service, traffic management and car parking facilities.I am delighted that the project will also deliver enhanced sports facilities for community use outside school hours.  This has been made possible through additional investment of around £4m which is being provided by Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council.I have no doubt that this fantastic project will deliver enormous benefits for the whole community.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any work undertaken by their Department, or in conjunction with the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, to review or modernise equality legislation, in line with the Equality Act 2010.
answer_text: There are extensive legal protections in place in respect of equality in this jurisdiction. Whilst there is no consensus on a single Equality Bill, work is ongoing to enhance the legislative framework. This includes the review of the Race Relations (NI) Order 1997.  Junior Ministers have also met with Claire Sugden MLA to discuss her proposed Age (Goods, Facilities and Services) Bill. Additionally, the Committee for The Executive Office is examining gaps in equality legislation here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of deafblind social workers there are within the health service, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Table 1 below outlines the number of Social Workers in each of the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts that work specifically with individuals who are deafblind.  It is important to acknowledge that deafblind individuals are not defined by their sensory support needs specifically and like all other members of society may require social work intervention and support for various reasons.  Within this context, social workers situated across the broad spectrum of specialist services for children and adults may all work with individuals who are deafblind, depending on their presenting primary assessed need at a particular time.  Specialist sensory support services and social workers with experience working with deafblind individuals are in a position to offer bespoke advice, or direct input in such circumstances as and when required.Table 1 –TRUSTTotal Number of Social Workers Working with deafblind individuals SHSCT 7 Social WorkersNHSCT13.5 Social Workers SEHSCT7 Social WorkersWHSCT10 Social Workers BHSCT8 Social Workers

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the delivery of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme.
answer_text: Delivery of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme has continued in line with the timeline published in January 2025. This has included Year 2 of the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme, the Farming for Sustainability – Knowledge Scheme, the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment, the Dairy Carbon Networks project, Year 1 of the Suckler Cow Scheme, the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme Zone 4, Year 1 of the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme and the Horticulture Pilot Scheme.The commencement of the Farming with Nature Package was a key priority for me this year. I am encouraged by the good uptake of the first strand of this Package, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, demonstrating that the demand exists within the farming community to do more for nature.  This year also saw the introduction of the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment (FSTP) that replaced the Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) with minimal changes, retaining the minimum claim size at 3ha and introducing the historic years exercise as part of the Farm Sustainability Payment that is planned for introduction in 2026.Alongside those SAP schemes which have already commenced, policy development for future schemes is ongoing in collaboration with industry stakeholders. A supporting programme of communications is also in place to encourage Scheme participation.The SAP timeline, that is subject to necessary business case agreements and legislation, can be viewed on the Department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an updated timeframe for the ongoing closure of Bann Bridge, Kilrea.
answer_text: I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and the understandable frustration of the local community, including businesses in the area and I apologise for the inconvenience caused.  My Department is working at pace to develop a temporary solution which will allow the bridge to be reopened as soon as possible.  Since the closure on 20 September, my Department has completed ground investigation, a detailed design, and issued a tender package. I can confirm that the procurement of a specialist contractor is underway with the aim of starting repairs as soon as possible. I expect to be in a position shortly to confirm the timeline for both the repair works and the reopening of Kilrea Bridge.To keep the public updated on progress a dedicated webpage for Kilrea Bridge has been created on the Department’s website that can be found at Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure | Department for Infrastructure. This page will be regularly updated as further information becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether encompass can automatically flag if a patient is deaf and in need of a British Sign Language or Irish Sign Language translator.
answer_text: Encompass includes a flagging feature for patients or service users who require British Sign Language or Irish Sign Language interpretation. Notifications are triggered at key stages of the patient journey, such as during appointment scheduling, to alert professionals of the need for interpreter support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department is supporting those businesses in Rasharkin, Kilrea and the neighbouring area, impacted by the closure of Bann Bridge.
answer_text: Responsibility for road and bridge safety and maintenance lies with the Department for Infrastructure and I understand that Causeway Coast and Glens Council are actively engaging with Department for Infrastructure on this issue. Invest NI has been engaging with their client businesses in the Kilrea / Rasharkin area to determine if they have been adversely affected by the bridge closure. While none of those businesses have raised concerns, I appreciate that this closure, and the lengthy diversion as a result, has caused issues and inconvenience for some local residents and businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the plans her Department has to minimise the disruption to local residents and businesses, due of the closure of Bann Bridge, Kilrea.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge the inconvenience caused by the closure and the impact it has had on the local business and residential community, particularly with regard to increased travel times for commuters and disruption to public transport services in the area.My Department is working at pace to take forward temporary repairs to allow the Bann Bridge in Kilrea to be reopened as soon as possible. Officials are also reviewing the traffic management arrangements in and around Portglenone to identify potential improvements that could help ease traffic flow along the diversionary route. To keep the public informed of progress, a dedicated webpage for Kilrea Bridge has been established on the Department’s website Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure | Department for Infrastructure. This page will be regularly updated as further information becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how the findings of new research by the Simon Community report on Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, will be used to inform the development of the new homelessness strategy.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As part of the development of the Homelessness Strategy 2027-32 the Housing Executive will engage with the Simon Community, as part of their membership and contribution to the Central Homelessness Forum, in considering the findings of the recent report on Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle.The Housing Executive will also work with Simon Community and the Central Homelessness Forum in considering the findings of this report as we shape the actions for 2026/27 of the current strategy, and specifically, the Youth Homelessness Action Plan.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of social workers in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust with a qualification in sign language, broken down by level.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they do not hold the relevant information pertaining to these questions and therefore are not able to report. I can confirm that my Department commissions a regional communication contract for individuals who are d/Deaf and d/Deafblind.  This contract provides sign language interpreters both on a face to face basis and remotely via video link across 24 hours a day, 7 days per week.  The interpreters are accessed via the Health and Social Care staff for all appointments and communications with individuals who use British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language as their first language.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of employees in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust with a qualification in sign language, broken down by level.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they do not hold the relevant information pertaining to these questions and therefore are not able to report. I can confirm that my Department commissions a regional communication contract for individuals who are d/Deaf and d/Deafblind.  This contract provides sign language interpreters both on a face to face basis and remotely via video link across 24 hours a day, 7 days per week.  The interpreters are accessed via the Health and Social Care staff for all appointments and communications with individuals who use British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language as their first language.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to ensure that young people in rural areas have equal access to safe spaces and structured youth provision.
answer_text: My department’s current policy for youth work in education, Priorities for Youth, sets out the requirement for the Education Authority (EA) to undertake an assessment of need to inform their Regional Youth Development Plan.  This assessment covers the needs of all young people including those in rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider adding (i) St Olcan's Primary School, Armoy; (ii) St Patrick's Primary School and Nursery Unit, Loughguile; and (iii) Gaelscoil An Chaistil, Ballycastle, to the winter gritting schedule, in light of the particularly dangerous conditions experienced outside these schools last year.
answer_text: The salted network is 7,000km long, covering 28% of the road network and facilitating approximately 80% of traffic. This network has been developed in line with my Department’s Winter Service Policy RSPPG E022.Details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via the following link; DFI Roads Salted Network.Unfortunately St Olcan’s Primary School, Armoy; St Patrick’s Primary School and Nursery Unit, Loughguile; and Gaelscoil An Chaistil, Ballycastle do not meet the qualifying criteria for inclusion in the schedule of salted routes.Schools affected by snow or ice on the adjacent network can request a salt bin and/or grit piles on the shortest route connecting the school to the salting schedule, for use on a self help basis.In relation to Gaelscoil An Chaistil, Ballycastle, I can advise that Kilns Road / Mill Street will be treated, as part of the diversionary route, for the duration of the Fairhill Street Road closure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the engagement her Department has had with young people in rural areas, regarding transport provision to and from youth services, over the last two years.
answer_text: My Department does not directly engage with young people in rural areas regarding transport provision.  However, Translink set up the Translink Youth Forum in 2009 to provide a supportive and enabling mechanism for children and young people to give their views, share their experiences, identify their needs and contribute their ideas on passenger transport in the North.  Children and young people’s participation and voice are important in the delivery of Translink services.  Translink has made it a key priority to better understand the needs and expectations of young people. Representing almost 40% of its customer base, Translink wants young people to be proud of their public transport services and for Translink “To be Your First Choice for Travel in the North”.  Other ways that Translink engages with young people is with Team Translink’s engagement at schools, colleges and universities on a weekly basis. They provide an opportunity for Team Translink to have open conversations with young people, and they highlight any notable feedback and opportunities back to the Youth Forum to discuss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessments made of childcare availability in rural areas; and (ii) how findings have been factored into the development of a childcare strategy.
answer_text: In 2023, my department commissioned RSM Consultancy to conduct a review of childcare services. The review sought to provide a current, comprehensive, evidence-based assessment of the childcare sector in Northern Ireland, including an analysis of supply and demand for childcare provision and identification of gaps in provision by geographical area, including specific consideration of rural areas. The report is available at: RSM (2023) DE Review of Childcare Services in NI - Final Report.PDF.In 2024/25, my department commissioned the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to carry out the Northern Ireland Childcare Survey. The first release of Official Statistics on the Childcare Survey were published on 8 May 2025. The publication presents insights on a range of issues including the type and amount of childcare used; childcare costs; summer holiday childcare arrangements; and factors influencing parental childcare choices, including affordability, quality of care, and proximity. The survey’s findings can be accessed at: Childcare Survey | Department of Education.The findings from these reports, including the issues raised regarding availability of childcare in rural areas, have been used to inform the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I will bring forward to the Executive in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support her Department is providing for low-income households struggling with high energy costs, particularly those in rural areas which are not on the gas grid.
answer_text: The Executive’s strategy – the Path to Net Zero Energy, aims to move away from the imported fossil fuels that put us in the position of a “price-taker” while ensuring access to essential and affordable energy for all.  The future net-zero energy system will be transparent, fair, and inclusive of all consumers and communities, including those people on low incomes or in rural communities. There is funding available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) to install household energy efficiency measures, enabling lower income households to reduce energy consumption and carbon emissions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how her Department is prioritising safe walking and cycling routes to (i) schools; and (ii) community facilities, in rural areas.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools, predominantly in rural areas across the North, and are listed on my Department’s website, link; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also currently carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review. Street lighting can also contribute significantly to feelings of personal security and safety, particularly for women and young people travelling at night. This is an important consideration when officials are developing proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling facilities to key destinations with the aim of encouraging modal shift. My Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting in rural locations considers:-The density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant nighttime use; and, Road safety, where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night-time collisions.I will continue to consider any steps my Department and our road safety partners can take to help reduce the number of people killed and injured in road traffic collisions. However, there are actions we can all take that will help. I would urge all drivers to take care on the roads, slow down, ignore mobile phones and never drive under the influence of alcohol or drugs. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support her Department is providing to enable parents in rural areas to access flexible employment opportunities.
answer_text: I understand the challenges faced by parents in rural areas in accessing flexible employment and my Department is involved in a range of supportive measures which contribute to boosting workforce participation and supporting families. On 28 April 2025, I announced my intention to modernise the employment law framework in the north of Ireland through my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill. This includes an enhancement of the right to make a statutory flexible working request for all employees from the first day of employment and increasing the number of permissible statutory requests within a rolling 12 month period to two. These changes will benefit parents in rural communities. These important legislative developments will make a significant contribution to my economic vision, promoting regional balance and creating good jobs, and will allow individuals to balance their work with their family and private life.The recent completion of Project Stratum has improved the availability of broadband services to over 81,000 premises including open countryside and small villages with population less than 1,000 people.Invest NI’s Alternative Talent Pathways initiative supports local businesses to identify and engage with groups who are under-represented in the labour market to streamline pathways to sustainable employment. In addition, the Labour Relations Agency continues to highlight to employers the options and benefits of flexible working.Local Economic Partnerships (LEPs) are a critical part of my regional balance agenda, and eleven Partnerships have now been set up across the North. My Department and Invest NI will continue to work closely with the Partnerships to develop action plans which they believe will have a positive impact on regional economic disparities and regional balance, including those in rural areas. My Department’s range of skills initiatives include online courses, delivered through both further and higher education institutions, which offer increased accessibility for parents in rural areas to create progression pathways to flexible employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how rurality is factored into funding allocations for youth services.
answer_text: My department’s policy for youth work in education, Priorities for Youth, sets out the requirement for the Education Authority (EA) to undertake an assessment of need to inform its Regional Youth Development Plan.  Funding allocations are made based on this plan and the EA’s funding formula.The EA’s current funding formula allocates resources on the following basis:Deprivation – 55%Total Population – 10%Target Age Range Population – 15%Rurality – 20%

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how Home to School Transport policies support families in rural areas, where there is no safe walking route.
answer_text: The purpose of my Department’s Home to School Transport policy is to facilitate pupil’s attendance at school and ensure that all children can attend a suitable school, regardless of where they live. The Policy provides vital support for rural families by facilitating funding for bus passes which in turn supports the provision of Translink bus services in rural areas. In locations where such services are unavailable, the Education Authority (EA) arranges transportation either through its own fleet or by contracting private operators. Where parents feel that a route is not safe to walk when accompanied by an adult, they may raise these concerns with the EA. The EA has a process in place to respond to concerns over the safety of individual roads and a parent can submit a road safety appeal if they feel their child’s route is unsafe to walk. The EA will assess the route in line with Road Safety GB ‘Assessment of Walked Routes to School’ guidelines, appointing an independent road safety engineer if necessary. I recently met with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Education Authority to discuss the safety of children on their journey to school. The safety of footpaths is within the remit of the Minister for Infrastructure; however, my Department and the EA will provide any support needed to develop measures to make the journey to school safer for children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of rural post-primary schools which currently have access to a funded, school-based counselling service; and (ii) any plans there are to extend this provision.
answer_text: The HOPE post-primary counselling and therapy service is available to all mainstream post-primary schools, EOTAS provisions and special schools that have post-primary aged pupils.  Further information can be found on the Education Authority website (https://www.eani.org.uk/services/pupil-health-and-wellbeing/school-counselling-and-therapy-hope-service).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any consideration has been given to a developing a dedicated rural youth strategy.
answer_text: My department’s current policy for youth work in education, Priorities for Youth,  includes a commitment that targeted provision will be supported to help meet the needs of specific groups of young people, including those living in rural communities.  There is no evidence to suggest a dedicated rural youth strategy is required.The Education Authority has the statutory responsibility for the provision of youth services.  To support rural youth work, the Education Authority Youth Service (EAYS) has developed guidance, entitled ‘Working with Young People in Rural Areas’, along with key youth work providers across Northern Ireland in both statutory and voluntary settings.  The EAYS also held two stakeholder engagement events to support the development of rural youth work.EAYS also actively engages young people from rural areas when it develops its Regional Youth Development Plan (RYDP).  The latest survey undertaken as part of the RYDP for 2026-2029 included survey responses from children and young people, 30% of whom were from rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessments that have been carried out on the availability of extracurricular and after-school activities for young people in rural areas.
answer_text: The Extended Schools Programme (ESP) represents the largest provision of after-school activities funded by my Department. There are currently over 120 schools in rural areas participating in the ESP. The Education Authority (EA) produces annual ESP reports that provide summary information and evaluations of programme delivery in schools. However, no specific assessments have been carried out to evaluate delivery in rural areas.Outside of the ESP, the provision of extracurricular and after-school activities is a matter for individual schools. Neither the EA nor my Department collects data on these activities, so no assessment of their availability in rural areas has been carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any specific support being provided to address youth mental health needs in rural areas.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline how waiting times for the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service in rural areas compare to urban areas.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what youth addiction intervention services are available in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Within the Northern Trust, youth addiction intervention services are provided by Step 3 CAMHS (Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service) Drugs and Alcohol Mental Health Team.  The team provides assessment and targeted therapeutic intervention for children and young people who present with significant substance misuse problems. Alongside this, the team offers support and consultation to practitioners both internal to CAMHS and externally within the communities as a form of outreach.This team aims to provide high quality, evidence-based assessment, support and intervention by working collaboratively with the young person, their family/carers. One of the key aims of the team is to provide a safe, equitable and accessible service for children and young people, and their families, most in need of the drug and alcohol mental health support.Referrals to Drugs and Alcohol Mental Health Team (CAMHS, Step 3) can be made by any healthcare, social work or education professional or voluntary/community agencies through the Children’s Referral Service (CRS).The Public Health Agency also highlighted a number of services available in NHSCT as follows:Community Based Services for Young People who are identified as having Substance Misuse difficultiesThis service provides Step 2 treatment services including psychotherapeutic interventions (structured talking therapies e.g. cognitive behavioural therapy, motivational interventions, structured counselling) for children and young people, aged 11-25 years including structured family support.  Therapeutic Services for Children, Young People and Families Affected by Parental Substance MisuseThis service provides therapeutic interventions and support to children affected by parental substance misuse as part of a multi-agency care plan through working directly with the young people and indirectly with non-substance misusing parents/carersTargeted Prevention services for Young PeopleThis service develops and delivers age appropriate drug & alcohol life skills/harm reduction programmes for young people in the age ranges of 11-13 years, 14-15 years and 16+ years across Northern Ireland.  Youth Engagement Services Eight Youth Engagement services for young people aged 11 – 25 years are available across Northern Ireland. The service provides up to date objective information about personal health and wellbeing issues (including drugs and alcohol), choices, where to find help / advice and support to access services when they are needed. There are two Youth Engagement Services in the Northern HSC Trust area in Ballymena and Carrickfergus.Substance Misuse Liaison ServicesCommissioned by both the PHA and SPPG this service is in place within acute hospitals in-patient settings (in particular, admission wards and Emergency Departments) across the 5 HSC Trusts. The service focuses on harmful/hazardous substance misuse among acute hospital admissions (non-dependent) and not requiring access to specialist addiction-specific interven

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline measures to incentivise rural GP practices.
answer_text: GP practices are coming under increasing pressure in areas where recruitment has traditionally been challenging including those in rural settings. In order to address this issue, my Department have developed the Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme to promote recruitment in hard to reach areas.The Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme aims to support practices in attracting and recruiting new GPs as well as retaining those who join their team.  GP practices will be able to apply for a number of packages under the scheme which include:•	A Golden Hello•	Relocation Costs•	Recruitment Costs•	Management Costs•	Reimbursement Costs for applying to the NI Performers List•	Funding for International GPsSince its establishment in January 2023, 106 GP practices have applied and received approval in principle for funding under the scheme.  65 practices have applied and received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP.GP Practices all receive a Global sum payment as major element of their practice income.  Calculation of each practices individual Global Sum amount is weighted for various components.  One of these components is rurality.  The funding is adjusted to reflect costs associated with delivering GMS services in rural areas for example distance from the nearest A&E, nearest from a city centre and percentage of patients living 3 or more miles away from practice.The minor injury enhanced service is a service which can be signed up to by rural GP Practices.  This service aims to provide a local minor injuries service in rural areas where patients do not have easy access to an existing provider of services (i.e. Accident & Emergency departments

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress to provide a new mental health facility in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Trust received outline business case approval in 2020 for the future provision of mental health services within the Northern Trust. The facility will be known as the Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health.The Trust has completed RIBA stage 3 design and in line with initial approval, has completed an addendum to the original business case that will be submitted to the Department of Health (DoH) later this month. Once approved this will allow the Trust to move to the next stage - RIBA stage 4 design in advance of tender and full business case.  Following the announcement of the outcome of the 2025 UK wide Spending Review in June, the Department of Finance has commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30. Whilst I remain hopeful, my ability to commit to funding capital projects, including the important Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health, will depend on the Department of Health capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any initiatives her Department currently have available in rural areas, to increase digital literacy.
answer_text: My economic vision is framed around the four goals of regional balance, productivity, good jobs and achieving net zero. Digital skills courses are delivered throughout the region. These pathways support upskilling and reskilling, from Essential Skills, Traineeships and Apprenticeships through to higher education including Higher Level Apprenticeships, degree-level and postgraduate study.  Further Education (FE) colleges embed digital literacy across mainstream programmes and offer dedicated provision, including short courses, Essential Skills Digital ICT, and community-based outreach. Traineeships for example, are available across a wide range of sectors, including Digital Skills and ICT.  In 2023/24, ICT was the 9th most popular traineeship, with 105 new starts. All traineeships embed digital literacy, equipping learners with the baseline skills needed for work and progression. With 29 campuses located across Northern Ireland, FE colleges ensure that learners, including those in rural and remote areas, can access digital learning opportunities. Their local presence, combined with targeted outreach, helps to overcome barriers to access and supports people in building the digital skills they need for work, study, and everyday life. Unless exempt due to existing qualifications, apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme are required to undertake the new NI Digital ICT Essential Skills qualifications which were introduced in September 2025. These standards set out the digital skills required by learners for everyday life and for work.  The ApprenticeshipsNI programme is delivered across the North by the six FE colleges as well as the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and 30 private training organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail GP to patient ratios, broken down by constituency.
