To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of households in rural areas that remain dependent on home heating oil; and (ii) any targeted assistance being considered for them.
answer_text: The most recent Northern Ireland House Condition Survey states that 84% of homes in rural areas are reliant on home heating oil as the predominant fuel source. The work on finalising the Fuel Poverty Strategy is considering the needs of those experiencing fuel poverty, including households in rural areas and those reliant on home heating oil.The Affordable Warmth Scheme is the Department’s energy efficiency scheme for owner occupiers and those in the private rented sector, including rural areas.  It provides a range of energy efficiency improvement measures for low-income households including cavity and loft insulation as well as new and replacement heating systems and windows where appropriate.Work is also underway on developing a new Warm Healthy Homes Scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends. It will provide support to low-income households experiencing fuel poverty, offering a range of measures including insulation, low carbon heating solutions and renewable technologies. The Scheme will be open to applications from all households that meet the eligibility criteria including those in rural properties that remain dependent reliant on oil heating.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any targeted support for off-grid homes reliant on oil heating.
answer_text: The Warm Healthy Homes Strategy 2026–2036 sets out a longterm, crossgovernment approach to tackling fuel poverty, including the needs of households that are off grid and reliant on home heating oil and other unregulated fuels. While there is no scheme targeted exclusively at offgrid oilheated homes, the Strategy includes a Strategic Action to research barriers and opportunities for more affordable oil payment options, including oil-buying networks, to assess ways of improving these for oil users. The Strategy commits to improve understanding of any supply issues experienced by those reliant on oil and solid fuel and ensure these customers are not forgotten or left behind. It further undertakes to work closely with partners as schemes launch to maximise take-up from rural areas where households are reliant on unregulated oil and solid fuels. My Department delivers the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) for owner occupiers and those in the private rented sector most at risk of fuel poverty. The scheme is directed at low-income households and not specifically targeted to off-grid homes reliant on oil heating. It provides a range of energy-efficiency improvement measures including cavity and loft insulation, new and replacement heating systems and windows, where appropriate.	 	Work is also underway on developing the new Warm Healthy Homes Fund that will replace the AWS. It will provide support to low-income households experiencing fuel poverty, offering a range of measures including insulation, low carbon heating solutions and renewable technologies. The Fund will be open to applications from all households that meet the eligibility criteria, including those off-grid homes reliant on oil heating.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service, broken down by Health and Social care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the impact of the £3.5m allocation to tackle gynaecology waiting lists.
answer_text: As part of the elective care funding & implementation plan £3.5m has been earmarked for capacity building for red flag and time critical gynaecology.   In-year funding continues to support non-recurrent additionality from both Trusts and the Independent Sector while recurrent capacity is being secured. Between April and July 2025, 1968 women were assessed or treated using waiting list funding, costing £924, 951, with more activity planned.There are ongoing discussions between the Department, Trusts and the Gynae Provider Collaborative around the medium to long-term investment requirements of Gynaecology. All parties are working to reduce red flag and time critical waiting times for patients, as well as to provide a more robust and resilient gynaecology service in the longer term. Led by clinician teams, the ongoing work will regionally standardise clinical criteria, develop clinical pathways that stretch from primary care to tertiary centre, support clinical e-triage processes, reducing variances in performance, as well as developing the nurse practitioner role and the role of gynaecology hubs.  These actions are all consistent with the GIRFT recommendations ensuring patients are managed in the right place, at the right time by the right professional.I can also advise that Gynaecology Primary Care Elective have been re-established to 100% capacity as of 01 April 2025. With an additional £1,073,986 cye funding being committed of the £2.9m recurrent funding.This additional investment will enable 6,072 patients to be managed by 16 Gynaecology GPs with Enhanced Skills across 18 Host Practices.As of 31 August, there have been 1,683 patients assessed and treated through this service. It is expected activity will incrementally increase with full activity to be delivered by 31 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how rural broadband coverage is being improved to support families with (i) homeworking; and (ii) children’s education.
answer_text: My Department has just completed Project Stratum, which has improved the availability of broadband services to over 81,000 premises. 97% of the premises in the Project Stratum intervention area are in NISRA Band H (open countryside and small villages with population less than 1,000 people). Project Gigabit is designed to provide access to gigabit-capable broadband where there are no commercial plans. The project is still in the procurement phase as my officials, along with Building Digital UK, are assessing new commercial rollout plans that became available over the summer. That assessment is due to be completed shortly, allowing the procurement to complete and a contract awarded. Details of premises benefitting from Project Gigabit will become available shortly afterwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of properties still not connected to fibre broadband, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: In May 2025 Ofcom provided an interim update to their Connected Nations 2024 report. This report provides a snapshot of mobile coverage and fixed broadband availability as of January 2025. The report, along with downloadable data on a constituency basis, can be found at: https://www.ofcom.org.uk/phones-and-broadband/coverage-and-speeds/connected-nations-update-spring-2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children currently waiting for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy procedures; and (ii) what the average waiting time is for (a) urgent; and (b) routine cases.
answer_text: Significant work has been carried out by my officials and staff within the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) to target the waiting times for PEG Tube surgery and to successfully reduce the time that patients are waiting for this very important procedure. As of the 3rd October 2025, RBHSC have advised there are 12 patients actively waiting for surgery. Of the 12 patients waiting, - 6 children are dated for surgery in October with 1 child classified as urgent;- 2 children are dated for November;- 4 children on the waiting list currently are not dated. These children have all been added to the waiting list in the later part of September.Of the 12 children actively waiting the average waiting time for:(a)	1 child (classified as urgent) is under 1 month; and;(b) 11 children (classified as routine) are waiting on average 3 months.13 children are currently suspended. One of these children is waiting for an MDT assessment.Whilst the above developments are to be welcomed my officials will continue to work with staff at the BHSCT in order to ensure that children who require this procedure can access the care they need in as quick a time as clinically possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Stratum.
answer_text: Project Stratum completed rollout on 30 June 2025, enabling 81,000 premises to access gigabit capable broadband (1,000Mbps). These predominantly rural premises had previously had download speeds below 30Mbps available.The project completed on time, on budget and to the agreed standard. At 94% gigabit capable coverage, the North now has the best coverage across these islands: England has 85%, Scotland has 78%, Wales has 76%, while the South has 88%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what action is being taken to ensure that children assessed as requiring urgent percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy procedures receive treatment within clinically-safe timescales.
answer_text: The Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) and the wider HSC has implemented a number of proactive measures to help address the backlog of children who require PEG tube insertions.  This has included RBHSC carrying out weekly theatre lists specifically for PEG tube insertions, securing additional capacity in Great Britain and the Ulster Hospital, for patients with less complex needs.  The Ulster Hospital have carried out 7 procedures. These initiatives have already led to a significant reduction in the waiting list from 92 children in April 2024 to 12 children actively waiting.  Eight of the twelve children waiting have dates booked for surgery, six children in October and two children in November 2025.  A further thirteen children are currently suspended from the waiting list due to social or medical reasons of which one child is waiting an MDT, these children are frequently reviewed by the paediatric surgeons.To increase capacity, the Trust has scheduled ongoing additional clinics and theatre sessions.Departmental officials maintain regular contact with Trust management to monitor progress and ensure that mitigation measures are being implemented.  PEG tube surgery is now a standing agenda item in the Regional Children Health Partnership meetings. Finally, I acknowledge that while progress has been made, the ultimate goal is zero children on the waiting list.  I must emphasise that sustained investment and coordination are essential to achieving this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an assessment of the impact on pensioners residing in the border counties of the Republic of Ireland of the policy that, from winter 2025-26, Winter Fuel Payments will not be payable to residents in the EEA Switzerland; (ii) an estimate of the number of those who may be affected by this policy who have links to Northern Ireland; and (iii) and any planned mitigations.
answer_text: (i) Whilst the Department legislates for the Winter Fuel Payment in Northern Ireland under the parity principal, Winter Fuel Payments are administered and paid by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP). DWP has confirmed that from winter 2025, Winter Fuel Payments will not be payable to those residing in the European Economic Area/Switzerland, including residents in the Republic of Ireland.For those living in border counties in the Republic of Ireland, this change means the loss of an annual lump sum (typically between £100 to £300 depending on age) that previously helped offset heating and energy costs in winter. The Department recognises that the change may impact the pensioners in border counties who previously fulfilled eligibility criteria which could result in reduced support to those with links to Northern Ireland but reside across the border. However, the provisions for payments in the EEA/Switzerland expired on 1 April 2025. From Winter 2025/26, Winter Fuel Payments will not be made outside the United Kingdom(ii) Border Counties - For winter 2023/24, before the payment was means tested, 26,230 payments were made in the Republic of Ireland – these residents may have links to England, Scotland, Wales or Northen Ireland. No further geographic breakdowns for the Republic of Ireland are available. (iii) There are no planned mitigations for those living in border counties in the Republic of Ireland who will not receive a Winter Fuel Payment following the UK Government’s change in policy around eligibility for payments to ex-pat pensioners living in the EEA/Switzerland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to the reintroduction of the universal Winter Fuel Payment in 2025-26, to detail (i) whether receipt of the payment will result in higher tax deductions for pensioners with taxable income above £35,000 who did not opt out; (ii) whether the full £200-£300 is recovered with no taper; (iii) whether recovery will be applied through PAYE coding or require Self Assessment; and (iv) the Department’s estimate of the number of Northern Ireland pensioners affected in 2025-26.
answer_text: Please refer to HMRC for an answer to this question;Please refer to HMRC for an answer to this question;Please refer to HMRC for an answer to this question;Based on NISRA projections, it is estimated that approximately 336,600 pensioners in Northern Ireland will receive the Winter Fuel Payment in 2025/26. Of these, approximately 48,000 – around 14% – will have their payment recovered by HMRC. This compares favourably with England and Wales, where approximately 20% of pensioners will be affected by the payment recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland will be evidenced for in terms of eligibility criteria for the Winter Fuel Payment, particularly for cross-border workers and pensioners temporarily in the Republic of Ireland during the qualifying week; and (ii) the appeals route where residency is disputed by the Department for Work and Pensions.
answer_text: There is no statutory definition of what is ordinarily resident. Each case is decided on its own facts. Whether an individual is resident or ordinarily resident is primarily a question of fact and degree. Ordinary residence means: (i) residence in a place with some degree of continuity, apart from accidental or temporary absence; or (ii) residence according to the way in which a person’s life is usually ordered.  For cross-border workers or pensioners temporarily in the Republic of Ireland, the key factor is whether Northern Ireland remains their normal home. Temporary absences (such as holidays or short stays) do not normally affect eligibility.If a person disagrees with a decision made about the Winter Fuel Payment they can ask for a mandatory reconsideration, seehttps://www.nidirect.gov.uk/appeal-benefits-decision#toc-1

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide (i) the Winter Fuel Payment overseas statistics due to be published on 16 September 2025; and (ii) any available breakdown of the 26,230 payments made in the Republic of Ireland in 2023-24 by nearest Northern Ireland border areas, by placing these statistics in the Assembly Library.
answer_text: The Department does not hold or publish any information with regard to Winter Fuel Payment claimants who reside outside of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the communications and mitigations in place to ensure pensioners are aware of potential HMRC recovery of the Winter Fuel Payment where taxable income exceeds £35,000, including (i) the date and content of guidance placed on NI Direct; (ii) which stakeholder organisations were notified; (iii) when they were notified; (iv) the number of Northern Ireland opt-outs recorded by 15 September 2025; (v) any advice provided to pensioners with fluctuating incomes around the threshold; and (vi) the routes of redress available where a pensioner believes the pay
answer_text: Where the information is UK wide, nidirect.gov.uk will signpost Northern Ireland citizens to further information available on the Gov.uk website. For this topic, a reference is published on nidirect, https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/winter-fuel-payment. This page has been published since 11 September 2025, superseding the page https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/winter-fuel-payment-centre. The previous page also advised that “Further information is available on Winter Fuel Payments”, redirecting to Gov.uk.(ii) & (iii)On 11 June 2025, key stakeholders including Age UK, Citizens Advice Bureau, and Independent Age were contacted to inform them of the 2025/26 Winter Fuel Payment changes and the 15 September Opt-out deadline. On 31 July 2025, DWP External Affairs contacted a wider group of third sector and consumer advocacy organisations with the same information. In parallel, HMRC contacted external tax stakeholders and Money Saving Expert highlighting the new income calculator and updated GOV.UK guidance pages referencing the Opt-out process. Money Saving Expert added a link to the new income calculator on its Winter Fuel Payment overview page.  Clear guidance was provided on how to Opt-out via GOV.UK or by calling the dedicated helpline.	The full list of organisations contacted:Citizens Advice Independent Age Age UK British Geriatrics Society British Red Cross Campaign to End Loneliness Centre for Ageing Better Charities Aids Foundation Citizens Advice Entitled To International Longevity Centre Money and Pensions Service (DWP Arm’s Length Body) Money Saving Expert NEST Oddfellows Pension Geeks Restless UK Rothesay Foundation Salvation Army Turn2Us Which.In addition, HMRC contacted:ICAEW (Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales) ATT (Association of Taxation Technicians) CIOT (Chartered Institute of Taxation) LITRG (Low Income Tax Reform Group) Money Saving Expert            (iv) This statistic is not currently available.    (v) As with any threshold, there will always be a cliff-edge with some pensioners losing out. However, this threshold means that the vast majority of pensioners will benefit from a Winter Fuel Payment. This is in line with the Government aim that more pensioners benefit from a Winter Fuel Payment from this winter. The threshold is also in line with average earnings and means those on lower and middle incomes will still receive payments, whilst ensuring payments are better targeted than the previous near-universal payment.    (vi) Any pensioner who believes that a payment has been incorrectly recovered by HMRC should contact the Winter Fuel Payment Centre. See https://www.gov.uk/winter-fuel-payment

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the disposal of the site of the former Northern Regional College campus in Ballymoney.
answer_text: Northern Regional College is actively taking forward the disposal of its former Ballymoney campus through Department of Finance’s Land and Property Service’s D1 process. The College has selected a preferred interested party and is currently working through due process to enable it to formally acquire the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions he has had with the Education Authority regarding the future use of the Balnamore Primary School site, following closure of the school in August 2025.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in undertaking engagement with stakeholders following the approval to discontinue Balnamore PS, received verbal requests to consider repurposing of the school building and to retain an EA presence in the area. This is in keeping with my request when approving Development Proposal 729 for the discontinuance of Balnamore PS with effect from 31 August 2025.   The EA has engaged with Sandelford School on the potential use of the former Balnamore PS building for SEN provision and the current plan is for Sandelford School to occupy the site from September 2026. The repurposing of the building will not only meet SEN need but make effective use of the Education Authority estate as the managing authority of controlled schools.The repurposing of the school building represents a positive future for the building and the surrounding area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Taoiseach and Tánaiste’s recent announcement of €20 million over 5 years from the Shared Island Fund for Arts and Cultural Heritage programmes.
answer_text: I welcome any funding opportunities that can have a positive impact for the Culture, Arts and Heritage Sectors in Northern Ireland.  Decisions on the nature of the programme are matters for the Government of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the rationale for the requirement to wait until week three of the five-week wait period before being eligible to apply for the Universal Credit New Claim Grant.
answer_text: Those migrating from a legacy benefit to Universal Credit receive a run on payment of their legacy benefit following the closure of their legacy claim.  The payment is made in week two of the five week Universal Credit waiting period, effectively reducing the waiting period to three weeks.  There is no requirement to wait until week three before applying for the Universal Credit New Claims Grant. However, applicants are encouraged to do so as a claim made at the end of the two week run on period will ensure that any assistance available is maximised and not reduced or negated by the run on payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the designation of Causeway Hospital as a diagnostic and elective hub for the North West.
answer_text: The Northern Trust’s stated ambition is for Causeway Hospital to operate as an elective and diagnostic hub for the North West.The Department of Health’s consultation document ‘Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes’ (Oct 2024) included Proposed Action 5: Consideration to be given to designating Causeway as an Elective Care Centre.The Trust has taken receipt of an MRI scanner for the Causeway site, and has submitted a bid to the Department under the Elective Care Framework to increase the volume of elective surgery provided in Causeway Hospital.I am considering the current proposals for Causeway, including designation as an Elective Overnight Centre, and will make my decision in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to engage directly with the Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media in the Republic of Ireland regarding the design and terms of reference of the Shared Island Cultural Cooperation funding schemes.
answer_text: I met with Minister O’Donovan on 9 April 2025, and the Shared Island programme formed part of the discussion. My officials liaise regularly with their counterparts on this, and other matters of mutual interest.  They will continue to do so.Decisions on the design and terms of reference of the Shared Island Cultural Cooperation funding schemes are properly matters for the Government of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is actively taking to promote awareness and uptake of the Universal Credit New Claim Grant.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that people are aware of the availability of the Universal Credit New Claim Grant which is unique to Northern Ireland and can provide important help to those awaiting their first Universal Credit payment. To ensure the range of support available is clearly explained, an entirely redesigned Finance Support NI Direct campaign page has been developed using stakeholder feedback and enables users to obtain information on all aspects of Discretionary Support inclusive of the Universal Credit New Claims Grant.  The NI Direct, Finance Support page can be accessed via the following website link www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-supportEnhancements have also been made to the Universal Credit journal which includes signposting and information on the Universal Credit New Claims Grant and how to apply as one of the initial messages a customer receives when they first claim Universal Credit. Specific signposting to the availability of the New Claims Grant is also included in the migration letter for those legacy benefit claimants currently being invited to claim Universal Credit as part of the ongoing Move to Universal Credit exercise. My Department has also promoted the availability of the Universal Credit New Claims Grant Scheme through its close links with advice sector colleagues and has cascaded awareness of the scheme though its wide range of Neighbourhood Renewal partnerships. In addition to the public information campaigns for claiming Universal Credit and the Move to Universal Credit, the Universal Credit New Claims Grant continues to feature within the Department’s digital advertising, with direct links to NI Direct for more information. I will continue to ensure my Department promotes the availability of the scheme to ensure people and families here are aware of the help and support available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Attendance Allowance recipients who report mobility-related challenges; and (ii) what proportion of these individuals would otherwise meet the mobility criteria under Personal Independence Payment.  .
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of the impact of the absence of mobility-related support for people who become disabled after reaching State Pension age.
answer_text: The Department has not undertaken any assessment of the impact of the absence of mobility-related support for people who become disabled after reaching state pension age.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Universal Credit New Claim Grants awarded since their introduction.
answer_text: The number of Universal Credit New Claim grants (formerly known as Universal Credit Contingency Fund grants until March 2025) awarded since their introduction in 2017, is set out in the table below;YearAwarded and paid2017/2018902018/20194,2302019/20204,8002020/20214,8002021/20227,6622022/20239,7402023/202411,1602024/2025*15,224**subject to final reconciliation checks prior to publishing

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has explored the feasibility of a joined-up, invest-to-save approach to mobility support for older disabled people, to reduce long-term demand on health and social care services.
answer_text: My Department has not explored the feasibility of a joined-up, invest-to-save approach to mobility support for older disabled people.  Both the Department of Health and the Department for Infrastructure offer schemes that may assist older disabled people in accessing appointments and activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department has given to reforming or supplementing Attendance Allowance to include a mobility component for those who become disabled after State Pension age.
answer_text: My department does not have any plans to reform the current policy for Attendance Allowance to include a mobility component. However, like all policies my officials shall continue to keep this under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the road improvement scheme on the Glenshesk Road, Armoy, following a landslide in March 2024.
answer_text: I can advise that the work associated with the significant embankment failure on the Glenshesk Road commenced Tuesday 18 March 2025.It had been envisaged, that with favourable weather conditions, work would be completed by the end of the summer.  Regrettably, due to difficult ground conditions and the quantum of illegal waste that must be removed from the site, progress on the scheme has been delayed.  Therefore, the road will need to remain closed with temporary traffic management arrangements and diversions in place until November 2025.My officials will continue to provide updates online as the scheme progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what oversight her Department currently has over funeral directors, including any role of Trading Standards or the Consumer Council.
answer_text: My department has no remit regarding regulation of funeral services, beyond the interest of the Trading Standards Service, which is solely from a consumer protection perspective and is covered under existing consumer protection legislation.  The Consumer Council has no specific statutory duty in relation to funeral services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what engagement her Department has had with the funeral industry regarding (i) regulation; and (ii) licensing, over the last five years.
answer_text: My department has no remit regarding regulation or licensing of the funeral industry, beyond the interest of the Trading Standards Service, which is solely from a consumer protection perspective and is covered under existing consumer protection legislation. Officials from my Department along with counterparts from the Departments for Health and Communities were briefed by the National Society of Allied and Independent Funeral Directors (SAIF) in 2022 about their work to roll out their Code of Practice to regulate funeral directors’ practices, introduced following a review by the Competition and Markets Authority published in December 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Irish Government regarding the Shared Island Fund allocation of €20 million over 2025-2030 for Arts and Cultural Heritage programmes, to deepen cultural cooperation, exchange and understanding.
answer_text: My officials engage regularly with counterparts in the Irish Government on issues of mutual interest, including in relation to relevant aspects of the Shared Island Programme.  Decisions, operational arrangements and announcements on the nature of the Programme are matters for the Government of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he has received clarification from the Irish Government on (i) how the announced €20 million Shared Island Fund allocation for Arts and Cultural Heritage 2025–2030 will be distributed; and (ii) how the €10 million competitive funding streams will operate.
answer_text: Decisions, announcements and any clarification specific to the purpose or operation of the Shared Island Fund are a matter for the Irish Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a Young Carers Festival in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the immense contribution that young carers make to their families and communities, often balancing their caring duties with education and other aspects of their childhood. Regrettably due to current financial pressures we are not in a position to offer additional funding for a festival at this time however should funding become available in the future I would be keen to revisit the proposal for a Young Carers Festival.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department will ensure (i) geographic; and (ii) sectoral balance in the allocation of funding through the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund.
answer_text: My officials are developing detailed guidance for the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund (NICIF) pilot scheme, and this will be made available when the scheme launches for applications in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund will support improvements to community buildings on (i) leased; or (ii) non-freehold land.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer for AQW 29405/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the steps he is taking to ensure the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund is accessible to groups operating in areas of high deprivation.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer for AQW 29405/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement has taken place with the voluntary and community sector in developing the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with a range of organisations during the initial phase of development of the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund (NICIF) pilot scheme. This includes sectoral umbrella and membership organisations, funders, other NICS departments and officials involved in delivering comparable schemes elsewhere in the UK and Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund will ensure compliance with duties under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.
answer_text: My Department has conducted an equality screening exercise and concluded that the proposals are not anticipated to have significant implications for equality of opportunity or good relations and, therefore, considers that an Equality Impact Assessment is not necessary. A copy of the screening exercise documentation is available on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given their contribution to the local economy, to outline any steps she is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland remains a welcoming and inclusive place for skilled migrant workers.
answer_text: Migrants are hugely important to our economy and to our public services. My Departments Skills Barometer has highlighted that we will have an undersupply of 5,400 individuals per annum needed to fill vacancies over the next decade and inward migration will be crucially important to filling this gap. As the member is aware, immigration is an excepted matter and is the responsibility of the British Government.  However, a range of my Department’s training provision is available to migrants with the right to work in the North, including Skill Up, Assured Skills academies and Apprenticeships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timeline for (i) publication; and (ii) implementation, of the action plans associated with the Anti-Poverty Strategy, including when (a) measurable targets, (b) delivery milestones; and (c) reporting mechanisms will be made publicly available.
answer_text: The draft Anti-poverty Strategy is currently undergoing public consultation until 19 September ahead of final Executive Agreement and publication. 	The final Strategy will include an action plan which will have clear delivery milestones and targets on putting actions in place to ensure that the Executive is delivering. The Monitoring, Reporting and Governance section of the draft Anti-poverty Strategy contains details on a proposed suite of measures and monitoring structures designed to track progress of the actions to tackle poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with the most recent research and policy recommendations from organisations such as (i) the Joseph Rowntree Foundation, (ii) the Trussell Trust, and (iii) the Child Poverty Action Group when formulating the Anti Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: The development of the draft Anti-poverty Strategy included consideration of a wide and comprehensive range of resources and research, including those referenced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of newcomer children recorded as absent from both primary and post-primary schools in the Ballymena area, on each day from Tuesday 10 June 2025 to Tuesday 17 June 2025.
answer_text: The department does not collect attendance data on a daily basis and therefore does not hold the information requested. In conjunction with the Education Authority, my officials continue to liaise with schools in the local area to offer support to those impacted by the recent disorder.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the supports which are currently being provided to schools with a high proportion of pupils from ethnic minority backgrounds, in the Ballymena area.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) activated its Major Emergency Plan arrangements in response to the impact of Civil Disorder on education settings and youth provision. This has provided a coordinated response across all required directorates.Throughout this period of unrest, the EA has engaged with the schools in the Ballymena area and wider school community. The EA has fully participated in the multi-agency coordination structures led by the PSNI to represent schools and youth provision.The EA has supported schools through direct engagement, holding joint Principal engagement meetings and the ongoing provision of information and guidance. This process has been led through the Intercultural Education Service (IES) and Education Welfare Service providing support for Newcomer families to access support and supporting schools with safeguarding and welfare concerns.IES provides advice, guidance and support to schools both around child and family support issues and teaching, learning and curriculum resources. The support staff include Social Workers and Teachers from IES who have helped schools engage with families and children who have not responded to phone calls, emails or letters from schools. This includes home visits (with free interpreters) to help confirm back to the school if the family is sheltering quietly, moved to be with relatives elsewhere in NI, or who have returned home to Bulgaria, Romania and other countries as an early term departure. The IES and other EA services are working with schools to plan for a return-to-work support plan from late August onwards to help schools deal with any additional wellbeing needs and reintegration support requests.In conjunction with the IES my officials continue to liaise with schools in the local area to offer support to those impacted by the unrest due to Roma children and other Newcomer children being either absent from school or worried about their safety in attending school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline why equal protection is not addressed in the Justice Bill (NIA Bill 07/22-27).
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any measures it is taking to make Parliament Buildings more energy efficient.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the potential impact on child poverty levels, following Trussells projections of an increase of 15,000 additional people being in hunger and hardship by 2029/30 following the UK Government’s disability benefit reforms.
answer_text: I recognise the seriousness of the projections published by the Trussell Trust. However, since the publication of the Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper and the introduction of the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill in Parliament, the UK Government has made several concessions which have lessened the impacts of its original proposals.In her Written Statement of 30 June 2025, Liz Kendall MP,  Secretary of State for Work and Pensions,  announced that new Personal Independence Payment (PIP) eligibility rules will apply only to new claims from November 2026, with protections to maintain the real-terms value of Universal Credit (UC) payments for those with severe conditions or nearing end of life. During the Bill’s second stage debate on 1 July, the UK Government committed to amending the Bill to remove entirely the clauses relating to the PIP changes, pending the outcome of the forthcoming PIP review. My officials have been working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to assess the proposed changes and their implications for Northern Ireland, and the impact analysis is available on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/universal-credit-and-personal-independence-payment-bill-impact-analysis-tables-30-june-2025.  The Universal Credit Bill: Universal Credit Rebalancing Impact Assessment dated 7 July 2025, which includes reference to Northern Ireland,  has been published by the UK Government and is available at https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/bills/cbill/59-01/0267/IA0707.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) his assessment of the recent decision by the UK Government to reverse its cuts to Winter Fuel Payments in England and Wales; and (ii) whether he has any plans to introduce a comparable uplift for pensioners.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Statement I made in the Assembly on 10 June 2025, Update on Winter Fuel Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an assessment of the likely impact on demand for local welfare assistance, food banks and crisis services, given the projection by Trussell that 15,000 more people will face hunger and hardship by 2029/30 due to the UK Government’s disability benefit reforms.
answer_text: The UK Government has decided not to progress the proposed reform of Personal Independence Payment (PIP) at this time. The revised Universal Credit Bill ensures protection for people already receiving the UC health element, and for new claimants who meet the severe conditions criteria or are terminally ill. The revised Bill also contains provision to increase the UC standard allowance above inflation from 2026/27 to 2029/30. Analysis of the projected impacts of the proposed changes in the Bill is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/universal-credit-and-personal-independence-payment-bill-impact-analysis-tables-30-june-2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any analysis his Department has made of the projection that 15,000 more people in Northern Ireland will face hunger and hardship by 2029/30 as a result of the UK Government’s disability benefit reforms, as modelled in the WPI Economics June 2025 briefing for Trussell.
answer_text: Since publication of the UK Government’s Green Paper ‘Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working’ my officials have been working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to assess the proposed changes and their implications for Northern Ireland. Following the introduction of the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill in Parliament, the UK Government made several concessions which significantly changed the impacts of its original proposals. This included a commitment to remove the clauses relating to the Personal Independence Payment changes in their entirety. It was also announced that the reforms to Universal Credit would impact only new claimants.Analysis of the projected impacts of the proposed changes in the Bill, as it stood on 30 June 2025, is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/universal-credit-and-personal-independence-payment-bill-impact-analysis-tables-30-june-2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on preparations for The 153rd Open in Portrush.
answer_text: Preparations for The 153rd Open have been ongoing for almost two years, supported by a number of multi-agency stakeholder groups. The build at Royal Portrush began in mid-March and is well underway, with main structures such as grandstands and the iconic leaderboards already in place. A considerable number of contractors and staff have been here for some time, staying and spending locally. The championship has generated over 3,000 local recruitment opportunities across functions including infrastructure, food and beverage and many more.Where practicable, the R&A and its suppliers use local suppliers with 80-85% of food and beverages sourced locally. Events of this scale boost the wider local economy and have a long-term effect beyond the event week.The R&A, in collaboration with Tourism NI and the local council, are delivering a comprehensive local community and business engagement programme as part of the preparations. Since February 2025 this has included community and business drop-in sessions in Portrush. These will continue up to and after the championship. The programme also contains online information including an industry toolkit, and in person industry events aimed at supporting businesses to make the most of The Open coming to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the affordability of the Barnahus model.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 14 - AQO 2077 22-27			   ANSWER A jointly chaired working group between the Departments of Justice and Health is considering a Barnahus model for Northern Ireland, as this is a shared objective of both mine and the Health Minister.    Whilst cost considerations will form part of ongoing policy development, at present the work is focused on looking at the needs of children and their families and assessing how current best practice, services and pilots, which follow Barnahus principles, can be built upon to design a model that best suits Northern Ireland.   I anticipate that the implementation of any model will require additional resources.  Future investment in children’s services will be necessary, and Barnahus provides a proven model for collaboration across services and effective use of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on discussions with UK Treasury on EU replacement funding beyond 2025.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will help to create more inclusive workplaces.
answer_text: The Good Jobs Employment Bill will help create more inclusive workplaces by making the right to request flexible working a Day-1 right, enabling two such requests in a 12-month period and removing the requirement for an employee to consider the impact of a request on their employer.  It will initially introduce up to 5 days of annual leave for unpaid carers to care for a dependent with a long-term care need. It is my aim to introduce a paid carers leave entitlement .The Bill will provide up to 12-weeks of paid leave for parents whose newborns require neo-natal care.  It will enable paternity leave to be taken as two separate blocks of one week, or as a single block; and it will enhance protections from redundancy and dismissal for pregnant employees, and for employees who have recently returned to work after a period of family-related leave. Ending exploitative contracts will ensure workers can rely on a decent and secure income, enabling more people to participate in the workforce. My proposals will also reform the way in which workers access and utilise workplace representation mechanisms, such as trade unions and information and consultation agreements ensuring that more workers have a say in those issues which affect them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an overview of how his Department will be engaging with the findings of report entitled The Prevalence and Impact of Adverse Childhood Experiences in Northern Ireland, commissioned by the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime.
answer_text: My Department acknowledge the work undertaken by The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) in commissioning this important report.  Officials work closely with EPPOC on the Department for Communities elements of Fresh Start work to embed a trauma informed approach.  My senior leadership team will give further consideration to how the findings of this report could be integrated across the Department’s wide-ranging work going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to rural-specific challenges identified in the Winter 2024-25 Northern Ireland Pulse Report, produced by the charity Sported, such as poor access to facilities and transport barriers for young people.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI is aware of poor access to facilities in rural areas and transport barriers to access the facilities that are available. Sport NI would note that due to the population density it is not always possible to have comparative sports facilities in rural areas as in urban areas. Sport NI would support better transport links from a sporting perspective; but would note that public transport in rural areas has a greater role than to solely facilitate access to sport and physical activity and that the Department for Infrastructure has responsibility for this.However, Sport NI have been operating a Programme, Your School Your Club, which provides funding to schools to develop their existing school facilities for opening to community use. This maximises the use of both existing facilities and existing public transport links in both rural and urban areas. Sport NI have also operated other programmes that were open to clubs and community groups across Northern Ireland to develop facilities to increase participation in sport and physical activity in both rural and urban areas”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.	I can also advise that a Rural Needs Impact Assessment process is followed at the inception of policies, programmes, standalone projects or major budget allocation decisions within my Department in order to ensure that rural needs are fully considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider establishing a dedicated funding stream for volunteer-led community sports groups, as recommended by those consulted in the Winter 2024-25 Northern Ireland Pulse Report, produced by the charity Sported.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI recognise that volunteer-led community sports groups are vital to Northern Ireland’s sports landscape. Sport NI have a funding stream that volunteer-led community sports groups can access, the Creating Opportunities Crowdfunder programme. This is open to all sports clubs, community organisations and charitable organisations. This initiative supports the development of new participation programmes, aiming to level the playing field across various sports by ensuring greater accessibility and inclusivity.This programme empowers grassroots organisations to secure funding, strengthen their offerings, and engage more participants. Creating Opportunities will be open to applicants, including volunteer-led community sports groups, in 2025/26 and 2026/27.For more information on the Creating Opportunities programme, including funding phases, eligibility criteria, and application deadlines, please visit the Sport NI website: Creating Opportunities | Sport NI. Sport NI would welcome applications from volunteer-led community sports groups.Sport NI is not considering a dedicated funding stream specifically for community sports groups; however, we remain committed to building capacity through existing programmes like Creating Opportunities. Furthermore, Sport NI has operated other programmes, that while not a dedicated funding stream for volunteer-led community sports groups, have been created and delivered with the intention of improving access to facilities and increasing participation in sport and physical activity within local communities. For example, Your School Your Club, provided funding to schools to develop existing school facilities that would then be made available for community use.As we enter the final year of our current strategy, we continue to collaborate with valued partners such as Sported. Together, we aim to support and explore further opportunities to enhance community sport in Northern Ireland.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of recent media reports, to detail any actions his Department is taking to ensure the validity of ADHD consultations in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: NHSCT have advised my Department that they are conducting a retrospective case note review of patients who were seen by Dr Anish within the Trust’s paediatric service during a specific eight-week period at the end of last year. This review is being conducted at pace and is ongoing. The outcome will determine what, if any, further action is required by the Trust.The Trust is keeping the Department informed while it carries out its review. Findings from this review will inform any action required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department became aware of the development and production of what became the UK Government’s Green Paper entitled Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working, published in March 2025.
answer_text: My Department became aware of the UK Government’s intention to make reforms to health and disability benefits following the announcement of the Autumn Budget by the Chancellor of the Exchequer on 30 October 2024. My Department subsequently became aware of the intention to bring forward a Green Paper following publication of the Get Britain Working White Paper on 26 November 2024.  Detail of the proposals contained in the Green Paper were not made available to my Department before 18 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following recent reports of school closures as a result of security concerns, to outline what support is in place for schools to ensure their security arrangements are appropriate.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Child Maintenance Service operates with formal guidance, internal targets or service standards in relation to providing a response to a child maintenance appeal once it has been lodged.
answer_text: Appeals on a maintenance calculation decision made by the Child Maintenance Service (CMS), are dealt with by The Appeals Service (TAS), within the Department for Communities. When making decisions in respect of appeals, CMS staff will reference the Department for Communities Decision Makers Guide for Child Maintenance. This guide is publicly available for review via the Department’s website. Chapter 48 is relevant to Appeals. https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/decision-makers-guide-child-maintenance-review-and-appeals Following lodgement of a valid Appeal by an appellant, through submission of a 'Notification of Appeal' application, The Appeals Service NI must respond within 3 working days confirming receipt.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will make a bid for capital funding for 2025-26 to enable and improve (i) accessibility; and (ii) health and safety, for arts working spaces and audience areas, including studios, theatres and office spaces.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland, as my Department’s Arms-Length Body responsible for the arts, provides advice to my Department regarding capital needs in relation to the arts sector. My decisions on the allocation of my Department’s capital funding budget which is provided by the Executive are made after analysis and prioritisation of various capital funding proposals in a very difficult budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of appeals (i) upheld; and (ii) rejected by the Child Maintenance Appeals Service in each of the last two years.
answer_text: Appeal hearings on maintenance calculation decisions made by the Child Maintenance Service (CMS), are dealt with by The Appeals Service (TAS), Department for Communities. All decisions made in respect of an appeal outcome are made by TAS. CMS provides all relevant material to TAS so a determination can be made, including the provision of presenting officers to represent the Departments position at hearing. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is considering implementing the recommendation from the Competition and Markets Authority that maternity units and baby clinics only provide unbranded infant formula to new parents so they are not influencing new parents to buy infant formula based on branding rather than price.
answer_text: Following the publication of the final Competition and Markets Authority report on the infant formula and follow-on formula market study, my Department is currently working closely with the Food Standards Agency in Northern Ireland to assess all of the recommendations. Officials are also engaging with counterparts across England, Scotland and Wales to consider the report.Until this assessment concludes, I am not in a position to comment on individual recommendations set out in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of child maintenance appeals lodged with the Child Maintenance Service (CMS); and (ii) the length of time taken to resolve child maintenance appeals lodged with the CMS to their final conclusion, in each of the last two years.
answer_text: Appeals on a maintenance calculation decision made by the Child Maintenance Service (CMS) are dealt with independently by The Appeals Service (TAS), within the Department for Communities. Upon receipt of a Notice of Appeal from TAS, CMS will prepare a response outlining the Departments position in respect of the maintenance calculation decision. This is known as the Departments submission. CMS have an internal target to respond to TAS within 12 weeks of the notice of appeal being received.	The Appeals Service NI does not have a standard operating timescale for dealing with a Child Maintenance Appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered introducing new incentives for employers who hire and support disabled workers through workplace adjustments and ongoing development opportunities.
answer_text: My Department funds the Access to Work (NI) and Workable NI programmes that provide incentives to assist disabled people who wish to take up, to retain and progress in employment, through practical personalised work based solutions. These programmes can also help employers who wish to recruit or retain people with disabilities in employment.My Department also funds the Condition Management Programme (CMP) which is a voluntary short-term wrap-around, multi-disciplinary healthcare provision that exists in Northern Ireland which provides the support to enable people with health conditions and disabilities progress towards, move into and remain in employment.Due to the constrained budget position and bids not being met in the 2025-26 budget, there are affordability concerns with introducing any new incentives for employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has formal guidance or service standards specifying the expected timescale for completing a child maintenance appeal from the date it is lodged until a final decision is issued.
answer_text: Appeals on a maintenance calculation decision made by the Child Maintenance Service (CMS) are dealt with independently by The Appeals Service (TAS), Department of Justice. Upon receipt of a Notice of Appeal from TAS, CMS will prepare a response outlining the Departments position in respect of the maintenance calculation decision. This is known as the Departments submission. CMS have an internal target to respond to TAS within 12 weeks of the notice of appeal being received.	The Appeals Service NI does not have a standard operating timescale for dealing with a Child Maintenance Appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any targeted support available to disabled people seeking to access apprenticeships and vocational training opportunities.
answer_text: ApprenticeshipsApprentices participating on my Department's apprenticeship programmes are provided with disability support to support their off-the-job training. Support is payable directly to Training Providers to help provide additional input of resources in terms of training time, equipment, or support for the participant to benefit fully from training. There are also programmes available through the Department for Communities to support people into and to sustain work and these can be put in place for any eligible apprentice.Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge FundWe have a strong apprenticeship offering which does provide training-related funding to support apprentices with a disability, but we recognise more can be done to increase participation from individuals with a disability. The Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund will provide up to £600,000 to facilitate collaborative projects across business, education and the voluntary and community sectors to derive innovative solutions, test new inclusion approaches and deliver key methodologies to tackle our inclusion challenges.This Fund will support a more diverse cohort of individuals, including those with disabilities, to undertake apprenticeships. It will be a key driver to connect more people across our communities with good job opportunities, promote regional balance and inspire life-long learning in line with my economic and social inclusion objectives.Skills for Life and WorkThrough the Skills for Life and Work Programme, my Department supports a cohort of young people who face significant barriers to engagement in work and learning. The programme supports young people to continue their journey towards further education, employment or an apprenticeship via vocational education and training focussing primarily on Entry Level and Level 1.Disability Support ServicesMy Department also provides assistance through the Disability Support Suppliers who work in conjunction with training suppliers delivering ApprenticeshipsNI and Skills for Life and Work. All participants are assessed and the support required will be provided by the Training Provider and additional specialist Disability Support provision.In 2024, my Department held a public consultation on options to support disabled young people on Skills for Life and Work. The outcome of the consultation has informed the new integrated delivery model.The Careers ServiceThe Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers, who work in collaboration with schools to identify and prioritise pupils with barriers and those who are considered vulnerable/at risk and most in need. This includes those with a disability, a medical condition and/or a statement of Special Educational Need (SEN).Building on a successful Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) project that tested innovative approaches to support young autistic people, the Careers Service is wo

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that skills and employment programmes are fully accessible and tailored to meet the needs of disabled people, including those with autism and learning difficulties.
answer_text: My Department offers a range of skills programmes, many of which include measures to support individuals who face additional barriers, including disabled individuals with autism and learning difficulties, to enable them to build skills and qualifications. Amongst these measures are Breaking Barriers in education, Skill Up and Step Up’s Disability Access Route to Employment and Education (DARE).  My Department also provides Disabled Students' Allowance to students in Higher Education who have extra course-related expenses as a direct result of a disability, mental health condition, or specific learning difficulty whilst a ring-fenced budget of £4.5m, per annum, is provided through the Additional Support Fund to Further Education colleges. This support ensures that the range of provision available is fully accessible to students with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement her Department has had with employers to (i) promote inclusive hiring practices; and (ii) ensure disabled people have equal access to job opportunities.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 24254/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department is ensuring that digital and technological skills training programmes are fully accessible to disabled individuals, including those with sensory and cognitive impairments.
answer_text: My Department offers a range of skills programmes, many of which include measures to support individuals who face additional barriers, including disabled individuals with sensory and cognitive impairments, to enable them to build skills and qualifications. Targeted support is provided by Higher Education (HE) providers through financial support and interventions including reasonable adjustments for, amongst others, those with a disability (physical, sensory or learning). HE providers seek to ensure their digital and technological skills programmes are fully accessible to all students. My Department also provides Disabled Students' Allowance to students in HE who have extra course-related expenses as a direct result of a disability, mental health condition, or specific learning difficulty whilst a ring-fenced budget of £4.5m, per annum, is provided through the Additional Support Fund to Further Education colleges. This support ensures that the range of provision available is fully accessible to students with disabilities, including those with sensory and cognitive impairments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has undertaken any review of existing employment support schemes to assess their effectiveness in improving job opportunities for disabled people.
answer_text: My department delivers a number of existing employment support provisions delivering interventions to support people with disabilities and health conditions to attain and sustain employment.The JobStart Scheme provides job opportunities offering 25 hours of paid employment per week at the appropriate National Minimum Wage.  Outcomes are reviewed regularly for all participants including those with a declared disability. This data indicates that the JobStart Scheme is effective in improving job opportunities for all including those with a disability. Between April 2021 and March 2025, 675 young people with a disability completed their JobStart Phase Two opportunity, of which 498 (74%) retained employment.Labour Market Partnerships (LMP) has recently been independently reviewed which concluded that LMP is filling gaps within labour market support.  Furthermore an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) for the 2023-24 DfC budget published in October 2023 concluded that “LMPs not only help those seeking work, but they are also a critical intervention to help people with a disability or health condition to remain in work”. One of the four priority themes for Labour Market Partnerships is disability which involves working with employers and participants to improve job opportunities. Between April 2022 to March 2024, LMPs delivered provision for 283 participants under the disability theme, 108 secured employment and 186 gained a qualification. Wider LMP provision also engages with people who have a disability outside of the specific disability theme.The Condition Management Programme (CMP) is a voluntary short-term wrap-around, multi-disciplinary healthcare provision that exists in Northern Ireland which provides the support to enable people with health conditions and disabilities progress towards, move into and remain in employment. Employability Indicators are used to measure the effectiveness of CMP which are assessed at the start and when a participant has completed the programme. The target is 95% to have increased their Employability Indicators as a result of participation on the programme. This target was met in 2023/24 and is on track to be met in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper acknowledges the rising number of young people with mental health conditions who are economically inactive, to detail any new mental health support pathways being introduced to help prevent young people from long-term benefit dependency.
answer_text: The ten year Mental Health Strategy, published in June 2021 by my predecessor, sets out 35 actions as a roadmap for the future delivery of mental health services. Actions 1 and 2 in the Mental Health Strategy focus on mental health promotion, early intervention and prevention.Under these actions, work is underway in partnership with the Department for the Economy to bring together representatives from universities, further education colleges and training providers to consider how to improve mental health promotion, early intervention and prevention for students and trainees. A small grants programme was put in place to enable additional supports and interventions to be delivered in those settings.  To date £160k has been allocated through small grants which has enabled the delivery of enhanced training for staff, wellness days, one to one counselling services, awareness raising workshops, meaningful activities, resilience programmes and toolkits and information resources.Grant recipients reported positive feedback from staff and students/trainees who took part in the projects funded, including improved wellbeing and understanding of their mental health; improved coping skills and self- management; improved relationships with peers and with staff; improved confidence about the future; and improved retention of trainees on training programmes.  Further to this, the Public Health Agency commissions a number of programmes which deliver early intervention supports for children, young people and families including: Early Intervention Support Service, a Family Support Team in each of the Trust localities aimed at providing a 12—14 week programme of support for families with emerging vulnerabilities aimed at addressing problems early and reducing risk of crisis escalation. The Service supports 650 families annually.Regional Mental Health Awareness Training Programmes, delivered directly to young people both in a school and a youth environment on a regional basis.  Targeted Prevention services for Young People, - this service develops and delivers age appropriate drug & alcohol life skills/harm reduction programmes for young people in the age ranges of 11-13 years, 14-15 years and 16+ years across Northern Ireland.  These programmes are delivered to young people identified as being at risk of substance misuse and are not delivered to the universal population of young people misuse (universal substance misuse education is delivered via schools). Youth Engagement Service, delivered to children and young people aged 11-25 throughout each of the five Health and Social Care Trust areas, to provide universal social and recreational services to children and young people aged 11-25 years as a platform for further engagement and to provide early and appropriate information and in-service support to children and young people aged 11-25 on the issues that are causing them concern.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken in the health service to reduce waiting times for treatment, particularly for disabled individuals and those with long-term health conditions that prevent them from working.
answer_text: The updated Elective Care Framework, published in May 2024, provides the strategic plan for the development and reform of elective care services over five years and details the measures needed to address long waiting lists. It aims to ensure that the Health and Social Care system is equipped to deliver an equitable, sustainable high-quality service for all individuals on a waiting list, including those with disabilities and long-term conditions.Although much more work is needed, progress has already been made on the implementation of a number of actions outlined in the Framework, specifically to increase activity for treatment. This includes:Continued development of elective care centres through regional Day Procedure Centres (DPCs) at Lagan Valley and Omagh Hospitals, orthopaedic day procedures at Duke of Connaught Unit at Musgrave Park Hospital and cataracts at Down, Mid-Ulster and South Tyrone Hospitals and elective Overnight Stay Centres (EOSCs) at Daisy Hill, Mater Infirmorum and South West Acute Hospitals.  Development of Post-Anaesthetic Care Units in Altnagelvin, Antrim Area, Belfast City, Craigavon Area and Royal Victoria Hospitals.Continued expansion of Rapid Diagnostic Centres at South Tyrone and Whiteabbey Hospitals and the use of mega clinics across all Trusts.Continued programme of Service Reviews across a number of specialties to drive forward ongoing improvements, including reviews of orthopaedics, paediatric orthopaedics, gynaecology, urology, general surgery, neurology and stroke.Work will continue in these areas to build on the good work to date and maximise capacity as far as possible to ensure timely treatment for patients. However, to meet the current demand / capacity gap an additional £80m per year recurrently is required. Without this investment our unacceptably long waiting lists will continue to grow. In addition, £135m per year for up to five years is required to address our elective care waiting list backlog. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget, as agreed by a majority of MLAs, no additional funding was provided for tackling our waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper, which outlined a £26 billion investment in the health and social care system to accelerate treatment and rehabilitation for those on long-term sickness benefits, whether he has any plans to invest in our health and social care system to ensure faster treatment and rehabilitation for individuals receiving long-term sickness benefits.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Rehabilitation Framework is currently under development. The progression of the framework, which is focussing on ‘partnering for better outcomes’ is being  led by the Chief Allied Health Professions Officer and her team as a workstream of the Advisory Committee for Allied Health Professionals. The Department has recently concluded an extensive stakeholder engagement exercise which will help inform the development of the framework and the final recommendations. The Condition Management Programme (CMP) works in partnership with people to help them progress towards, move into, return to or remain in paid employment, with the support of health professionals including occupational therapists, physiotherapists and mental health nurses. The key aim of CMP is to provide short term work-focused and educational interventions to help clients manage their own condition and re-focus them on their potential to return to work or remain in work. Work is also progressing on a new cross-departmental Executive Disability Strategy for Northern Ireland and, in parallel with this work, a Disability and Work Strategy is also being developed regarding employment for disabled people.  Both strategies are being led by the Department for Communities and my officials are fully engaged and participating in the co-production of the strategies.The new Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland will seek to support people with disabilities and health conditions to move into work.At present, there are no set/target launch dates for these strategies as the work very much depends on Ministerial/Executive endorsement and available budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any social care reforms that will be implemented to ensure that disabled people can live independently while receiving adequate support to enter or remain in employment.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to develop a 10-year strategic plan, which is intended to deliver reform of adult social care. The plan will build on the work of my Department’s Social Care Collaborative Forum established in April 2023. It will set a clear vision for adult social care in the future and will focus on a number of strategic priorities, including promoting and enabling individual choice and control. The plan will include a number of strategic actions aimed at enabling adults, including disabled adults, to live independently, in accordance with their wishes and in a home of their choosing.Day opportunities offer creative work-based placements and within day opportunities a wider range of provision is being encouraged to meet the assessed needs and enhance the potential of younger people transitioning from children’s services. This also includes those currently in day care settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will there be a dedicated cross-departmental approach between his Department, the Department for Communities, the Department for the Economy, the Department of Education and the health service to create a cohesive return-to-work strategy for disabled individuals.
answer_text: The Department for Communities is developing a new Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland, which will align to the Executive Disability Strategy currently in development.  In developing the draft Disability and Work Strategy, Department of Health officials have participated within co-production, led by Department for Communities, which has included the Departments for Economy, Education and Finance; as well as a wide range of stakeholders and those with lived experience of disability. The new Disability and Work Strategy for NI will seek to support people with disabilities and health conditions to move closer, into and progress in work. The Department of Finance continues to lead engagement with Whitehall on the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) and Post-2026 Funding. Part of this  work will help economically inactive people, including disabled people, into employment. It is supported by all NI Departments through the cross-departmental funding policy with the Interdepartmental Working Group.In addition, the Department for Economy’s Economic Vision seeks to close the employment gap between individuals with and without a disability.   At a more local level, my Department fully supports the provision of Day Opportunities which offer creative work-based placements and a wider range of provision is being encouraged to meet assessed needs.
