answer_text: My Department’s Analytics Division have confirmed the information requested is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the support available to train and upskill 18-to-22-year-olds living with a disability into the workplace.

To ask the Minister for Communities, following the publication of Action for Children’s new report, Paying the Price of Child Poverty, whether he will commit to ensuring that comprehensive cross-departmental measures to tackle child poverty are embedded in an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: My officials are aware of Action for Children’s report and will consider its findings in their work to develop potential actions to tackle poverty and child poverty in particular.Addressing poverty in all its forms is and has been a key priority for me. I remain committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland, through the implementation of a robust Anti-Poverty Strategy.The Anti-Poverty Strategy will aim to address poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty. In tackling child poverty we will focus on lifting families out of poverty and ensuring our young people have the best possible start in life irrespective of their socio-economic status.		Final decisions on the contents of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation prior to its final publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an assessment of wastewater capacity in North Antrim.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to ensure continuity of the Health Inclusion Nurse role in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidelines in place to support postpartum mothers admitted to general mental health wards.
answer_text: There are no specific guidelines in place to support postpartum mothers admitted to general mental health wards.In BHSCT, all post-partum mothers in the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre (AMHIC) are nursed in side rooms for privacy.  They are followed up by the Community Midwives and are supported during their admission by the AMHIC Multi-Disciplinary Team, which includes Psychiatrists, Mental Health Nurses, Social Workers (including Family and Child Health Social Workers), Psychology and Occupational Therapy. The AMHIC Team work closely with Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital, the SWAN (Social Wellbeing Antenatal Team), the Perinatal Mental Health Team and community teams (Mental Health, Midwifery and Health visitors) to provide holistic care and treatment to post-partum patients in AMHIC.The South Eastern Trust follow the Regional Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines that were agreed by the Regional Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathway network, PHA and SPPG on establishment of the service.The NHSCT recognises that there is clinical guidance in respect of antenatal and postnatal mental health: clinical management and service guidance. The SHSCT Bluestone Mental Health hospital, staff follow 2017 Regional Perinatal Pathway. This Regional Pathway is currently being reviewed by the PHA. At ward level, the Admission Checklist is followed for Perinatal patients.  Staff will also make contact with relevant teams including Health Visiting, Midwifery, Perinatal Mental Health Team, Breast feeding Nurse Specialist and Social Services if applicable.  A QI project commenced in May 2024 in relation to improving communication between Inpatient Mental Health Services and Health Visiting Services. This focuses on Silverwood, Cloughmore, Bronte and Rosebrook Wards. At the point of logging a referral for admission to the wards from Home Treatment Crisis Response or Approved Social Work Service, the Patient Flow Coordinator will ask about children under the age of 5 and will inform the Health Visitor for further support as part of our ‘Think Family’ practices. All wards have ‘Think Family’ Champions identified and a Family Visiting Room.  There is also a dedicated outside Child Visiting Garden.  Wards are also currently working on a project to develop ‘Keep in Touch’ Tools The WHSCT follow the Regional Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines that were agreed by the Regional Perinatal MH Care Pathway network, PHA and SPPG on establishment of the serviceThe Belfast Trust is unable to detail the number of (i) expectant; and (ii) six months postpartum mothers admitted to general psychiatric wards in (a) 2022; (b) 2023; and (c) 2024 by running any of our current reporting systems, as we do not record this information on admission. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure staff are trained to support the specific needs of postpartum mothers within psychiatric settings.
answer_text: The Regional Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathway was launched by the Public Health Agency on 5 March 2025. The new pathway provides clear guidance to all HSC professionals who care for women in the antenatal and postnatal period across all settings, inclusive of primary care.  Perinatal Mental Health multidisciplinary specialist teams will provide holistic assessments, planning and treatment, inclusive of a ‘whole family’ approach, for women with moderate-to-severe mental health issues from 12 weeks gestation, right up to one year following the birth.  These specialist teams are made up of psychiatric nurses, social workers, psychiatrists, health visitors, psychologists, midwives, and psychotherapists. This is to be implemented by each HSC Trust, with the Public Health Agency’s Regional Perinatal Mental Health Team, responsible for monitoring and updating the Perinatal Mental Health Pathway.Staff training is an operational matter and therefore it is the responsibility of individual Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure their staff are appropriately trained to meet the specific needs of individuals in their care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the budgetary provision for the remedial work on Glenshesk Road, Ballycastle.
answer_text: I can advise that £275,000 has been allocated in the current financial year for the B15 Glenshesk Road slippage of 27 March 2024.This funding has been used to facilitate surveys, progress the design process, and allow for commencement of the construction phase, subject to completion of the detailed design and favourable weather conditions.Further funding to complete the necessary repair works at Glenshesk, will be provided from next financial year’s structural maintenance budget once this has been finalised.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Truth Recovery Independent Panel.
answer_text: The work of the Truth Recovery Independent Panel is the first stage of an integrated truth investigation into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.  Its findings are vital to inform the forthcoming statutory public inquiry.This innovative approach was recommended by the Truth Recovery Design Panel following consultation with victims and survivors.  It is intended to provide a meaningful choice for those affected, on how they wish to have their experience included in the process.More than 100 people have already shared their testimony with the Panel, and a communications campaign is under way to encourage others who wish to share their experiences to come forward before 1st May.The Panel has also been working with PRONI and has identified over 4,500 archival records and has agreement to digitise more than 3,000 records.We met with the co-chairs of the Panel in November and were pleased to note the significant progress made to date. We are committed to supporting this process and those affected and look forward to the Panel’s report later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (i) who are currently assessed as requiring residential short breaks; and (ii) who access regular short break services.
answer_text: In response to these questions the Department of Health has provided information regarding all programs of care that this might be relevant to as no particular program of care was referenced in the question. Part (i) – number of people who are currently assessed as requiring a residential short breakWithin the HSC Trusts in adult services people are not assessed as requiring short breaks it is the carers who are assessed (through the carer assessment process) as requiring short breaks.  A “carer” is assessed as needing a short break, “to give them a break from their caring responsibilities” thereby a short break maybe provided to the person they are caring for.  It is important to note that residential short breaks are just one part of a continuum of provision Health and Social Care Trusts offer as alternatives to bed based care such as day care, a sitting service in the person’s own home, Shared Lives (Older People and Adult Learning Disability), Short Break Fostering and Direct Payments.In children services an assessment of the family situation is completed through the UNOCINI assessment framework to ascertain if short breaks including residential short breaks are required. Children with Disability Services have 66 children assessed as requiring a residential short break.Part (ii) – number of people who access regular short break servicesThe Northern Trust have provided the information for Quarter end September  2024 which is the latest information currently available in relation to ‘people’ accessing a variety of short breaks who are ‘carers’ and the number of children who are receiving short breaks.Older People 233 Adult Carers who received a short break Dementia 581 Adult Carers who received a short breakLearning Disability 207 Adult Carers who received a short breakChildren with Disabilities 305 Children are receiving a short break

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement his Department is having with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure equality of access to continuous glucose monitoring systems.
answer_text: The Regional Insulin Pump Service was established, with a departmental investment of £2 million, to support people living with Type 1 diabetes by enabling this life changing service. The Regional Insulin Pumps Service was co-produced with People Living with Diabetes, clinicians and health care professionals across all Health and Social Care Trusts, alongside Diabetes UK. The service goal is to provide equity, reduce variability and enhance outcomes for people living with diabetes across Northern Ireland. A regional multi-disciplinary team delivers the pathway, connecting with local Health and Social Care Trusts, to provide a high-quality service and ensuring equal access to insulin pump therapy.The most appropriate glucose monitoring system for each individual is the responsibility of the Health and Social Care Trust and their clinicians to determine, in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  Continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff can be provided.  NICE have published Technology Appraisal 943 (TA943) which recommends hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in type 1 diabetes.The Department has endorsed TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, however it is acknowledged that implementation will require consideration of the financial and service requirements, including clinical capacity, specialist support and procurement routes.Work is also required to ensure appropriate workforce arrangements to provide these products safely, effectively and equitably across the region.A clinical working group is being established to work with relevant stakeholders across HSC to develop an implementation plan with an appropriate timescale for rollout. This plan should provide equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to bring forward specific policies relating to the work of the National Museums of Northern Ireland, particularly within the context of the Heritage, Culture and Creativity Programme.
answer_text: A new policy for museums in Northern Ireland is currently being developed as part of a suite of policies within the Heritage, Culture and Creativity Programme which I launched in July last year. This work aims to develop a comprehensive policy that will apply across the entire museum sector, including National Museums NI, which plays a vital role within this framework. Upon completion of this process, National Museums NI will apply the policy when designing and developing their own strategic and operational priorities. My officials are working closely with National Museums NI to ensure alignment between their work on a corporate policy, and the work on the Museums Policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the criteria for identifying areas eligible for Communities in Transition funding.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any preparatory steps his Department has undertaken in relation to the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Bill.
answer_text: My officials have provided the Home Office with all the necessary support and guidance it requires regarding the element of the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Bill relating to liquor licensing and the disclosure of plans of premises.The Public Record Office of Northern Ireland already has a range of security measures in place. This includes: the presence of security guards during opening hours; an array of CCTV cameras providing external and internal coverage and staff areas that are only accessible using access control cards. Members of the public are required to provide photographic id to use the research areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Home Office in relation to the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Bill.
answer_text: I have received and have replied to correspondence from the Minister of State for Security in the Home Office regarding the element of the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Bill relating to liquor licensing and the disclosure of plans of premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of GP indemnity.
answer_text: I recognise that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally.My Department has been exploring options for the long-term future of GP Indemnity and I recognise that the pace of progress in this work has taken longer than expected however, this reflects the complexity of the issue.I had committed that this work would conclude with proposals for the long-term solution by the end of 2024.  I am pleased to advise that, after careful consideration of a wide range of options, I have identified what I believe is the best, most appropriate option to address this key issue for the long term.  As part of the 2025/26 contract negotiations, my Department has met with the Northern Ireland General Practice Committee and provided details of the long-term proposal for GP indemnity.  I am hopeful that we will soon be able to reach agreement on this important matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any funding and resources being allocated to support women in under-represented roles within sports or positions within sport; and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure greater gender equality in these roles.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any proposed usage of Balnamore Primary School following the decision to close the school at the end of the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: As a decision has been taken on Development Proposal (DP) 729 it is up  to the proposer in this case the Education Authority (EA) to implement the outcome of the DP in full. In the decision I made I expressed for the EA to give urgent consideration as to how this well-equipped school with good accommodation and facilities can be repurposed to consolidate controlled provision in the area and other educational needs that may exist.It is up to the EA to consider any future usage of the accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether preschool places in Balnamill Little Learners for 2025-26 will be impacted by the recent decision to close Balnamore Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has responsibility for allocating funded pre-school education places to non-statutory pre-school providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) and has confirmed that Balnamill Little Learners is not and has never been a participant in the PSEP.  The EA has confirmed that sufficient PSEP funded pre-school places are available within the area to provide a funded pre-school place for every target aged child whose parents want it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has had any discussions in relation to the Riverside Theatre, located at Ulster University, Coleraine, given its cultural significance to the region.
answer_text: I have not had any discussions with Ulster University in relation to supporting the Riverside Theatre.		The Arts Council is the funding and development body for the arts in Northern Ireland and the Riverside may wish to make  contact via info@artscouncil-ni.org to discuss funding streams which may be available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the engagement his Department has had in relation to the Riverside Theatre, located at Ulster University, Coleraine.
answer_text: My Department has not had any engagement in relation to the Riverside Theatre at Ulster University, Coleraine. However, my Department maintains an ongoing dialogue with the University and is open to discussion of any issue affecting the institution and its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the start date of the construction works on Glenshesk Road, Ballycastle, following two landslides.
answer_text: The B15 Glenshesk Road partially collapsed due to an embankment failure on 27 March 2024. The design process is nearing completion and when a final design is established, a construction programme will be developed and taken forward.  It is anticipated that a construction start date in spring of this year is achievable.In relation to the second slippage associated with Storm Bert, I can advise that the Glenshesk Road has been fully open at this location (Ballycastle side of Greenan Road), since 11 December 2024.My Department has provided a dedicated webpage to keep interested parties advised of progress on the Glenshesk Road reopening.  This can be viewed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/b15-glenshesk-road-ballycastle-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current criteria being used to decide which rural roads are included in a gritting schedule.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by the Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Departments website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks and even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed.As road users we all need to be mindful of the road conditions and adjust the way we drive, ride or cycle and during particularly wintry conditions people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps being taken to ensure that rural roads, particularly those leading to schools, are prioritised for gritting to ensure the safety of school communities during adverse weather.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by the Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Department’s website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed.The Department also has priority “secondary” salting to rural schools to ensure they receive a level of special attention throughout the winter period. Currently there are 80 rural schools not on the salted network, on the list for secondary gritting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement the Historical Environment Division has had with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council regarding the structural concerns of the listed site of the former Antrim Arms hotel in Ballycastle.
answer_text: My Historic Environment Division has been engaged with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council officials, the owner and his engineer since 10 October, when it was first advised of the structural issues.At that time, HED officials advised the council that it’s engineers report did not provide any recommendations to stabilise the building’s gable wall. Officials also advised the Council that specialist advice was required from a Conservation Accredited Engineer to assess the nature of the wall’s instability and to provide proposals for its stabilisation - or to determine that the removal of the gable was the only way forward. In addition, officials directed that - if demolition and replacement is deemed to be the only viable option - the Department will require proposals for the reinstatement of a replacement wall without loss of remaining building sections.On 25 October, HED reviewed options and provided feedback to the Council, advising that the proposed options were not deemed long-term solutions for the protection or repair of the listed building.HED officials are meeting with the owner on the 8 January about another listed property in his ownership and will seek to establish appropriate options/outcomes for the Antrim Arms building. In the context of legal action taken by the local authority against the owner, there is the prospect of further engagement, currently scheduled for March.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what budget has been allocated by his Department to address the safety and access issues on the Glenshesk Road, Ballycastle.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any discussions with the Housing Executive in relation to including a requirement on statutory authorities to ensure children are not moved a significant distance during the school year, in the forthcoming Refugee Integration Strategy.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any immediate action his Department is taking to address the significant public safety and access concerns on Glenshesk Road, following a second landslide in recent days.
answer_text: My Department is urgently assessing the extent of the damage to the road network caused by Storm Bert, including the B15 Glenshesk Road.I can confirm that at the site of the recent slippage, temporary traffic management, restricting vehicles to a single lane, is currently in place thus ensuring access is maintained.  It is envisaged that remedial works will be undertaken, as soon as practicable, to ensure access to the residents of Greenan Road will remain in place.In relation to the previous damage caused earlier this year, a detailed design solution is being developed for the embankment failure at Glenshesk Road.  It is anticipated the design process for the remedial works will be completed in early 2025, with construction works programmed thereafter, subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an updated timeline for the managed migration of individuals on legacy benefits to Universal Credit, including dates for when affected claimants in Northern Ireland will be notified and transitioned.
answer_text: My Department’s current priority is the migration of those households in receipt of Tax Credits to Universal Credit by April 2025.Announcements on the Department’s plans for its completion of Move to UC for those remaining in receipt of income-based legacy benefits, will be made in due course.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the impact the increase to the rate of employer National Insurance contributions will have on constituency staff budgets.
answer_text: Members are able to recover the costs of their support staff salaries under the provisions of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, within the annual maximum allowance. However, ancillary employment costs such as employer’s national insurance contributions, pension contributions and statutory redundancy payments are not charged against these allowances, but are met centrally by the Assembly Commission.  Therefore, increases to employer’s national insurance will have no impact on constituency staff budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline for improving access to advanced diabetes technology such as insulin pumps and hybrid closed loop systems for people living with type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.  Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, are being considered. Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered, as funding is required for devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline for when a clinical audit to measure the effectiveness of diabetes care will be introduced.
answer_text: The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales.The Department of Health remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  However, there have been significant challenges in progressing this recommendation, as the required infrastructure is not currently in place to participate fully in the audit.With the full implementation of Encompass, currently being rolled out across all Health and Social Care Trusts, the provision of data that is both available and extractable for the region will be available.  This will support participation in the National Diabetes Audit in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to ensure staffing levels are adequate to ensure that those who are currently unable to access annual check-ups with hospital consultants or Diabetes Specialist Nurses to monitor and manage their condition can access these services.
answer_text: A strategic workforce review for specialist diabetes, led by the Department of Health Workforce Policy Directorate, is proceeding as part of my Department’s ongoing workforce planning programme.  This review, which is near completion, aims to secure the workforce supply of those staff directly involved in the delivery of specialist diabetes treatment and care, including hospital consultants and diabetes specialist nurses. This 10-year forward view will inform workforce planning needed to ensure equitable access to specialist diabetes professionals across Northern Ireland, in order to provide a safe, effective and quality service for people living with diabetes.An Action Plan will be developed to deliver on this review’s recommendations.  I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of strategic planning workforce reviews that have been accepted by my Department.  However, we can only do this if we have adequate financial resources in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to the announcement in the Autumn Budget of a new Fair Repayment Rate to reduce Universal Credit deductions from 25 per cent to 15 per cent, will he consider going further and introduce a minimum income floor to ensure claimants can still afford to pay for essential items.
answer_text: I welcome the Chancellor’s announcement. The combined impact of the Fair Repayment Rate reduction along with the current Benefit Cap mitigation means that UC claimants in NI with deductions will keep a minimum of 85% of their UC personal allowance – a key recommendation of the recently published Joseph Rowntree report “A protective minimum floor in Universal Credit”.In terms of a minimum income floor, this already exists within Universal Credit and is only relevant to the calculation of self-employed earnings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
To ask the Minister of Education whether Operation Encompass can be used to support children and young people at risk from the threat of paramilitaries or child criminal exploitation.
answer_text: The Operation Encompass initiative relates to domestic abuse and cannot be used to support children or young people at risk from the threat of paramilitaries or child criminal exploitation.  The initiative is delivered under regulations that facilitate information-sharing in specific circumstances.  The scope of these regulations does not provide the necessary legislative cover to extend information sharing to children at risk from paramilitary activity or child criminal exploitation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans for jointly-commissioned services to engage in a single reporting protocol instead of multiple, different reporting procedures to various Departments.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sign Language Bill, including the policy area in his Department that will take this work forward.
answer_text: Given its cross-cutting nature, I have received Executive approval for my officials to engage formally with the Office of Legislative Counsel (OLC) for the drafting of clauses for a Sign Language Bill. I hope to introduce a Bill to the Assembly at the earliest opportunity which will recognise and promote British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language. This work is being progressed by the Sign Language Policy and Legislation Branch within my Department’s Culture Division.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on work to provide a pathway for sign language interpreters through enhanced training.
answer_text: My Department is currently investing in an Interpreter Training Programme delivered by Foyle Deaf Association which provides for the training and registering of six British Sign Language (BSL) and six Irish Sign Language (ISL) interpreters. As two students have left the programme, four ISL student interpreters remain.   In addition, my Department is providing funding to Queen’s University Centre for Translation and Interpreting to deliver a two-year MA in Interpreting for up to 20 Deaf and hearing students which launched in September 2024 and which will further boost capacity.My Department also financially supports students to acquire the linguistic qualifications necessary to access an Interpreter Training Programme to ensure a continued supply of suitable candidates.  However, the use of BSL and ISL interpreters by the Deaf community is an issue for all departments and public bodies and therefore will require resources in addition to my Department’s investment. I have highlighted this matter to my Executive colleagues, and my officials continue to work with other departments to quantify the requirements and how best to address them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his engagement with key stakeholders to develop a policy to inform the drafting of sign language legislation.
answer_text: My Department chairs and provides secretariat to the Sign Language Partnership Group which is a cross-departmental forum with representatives of the Deaf community which seeks to improve access to services for the Deaf community. Through this role my officials have consulted extensively with the Deaf community, other key stakeholders and Executive departments to develop the policy which is currently informing the drafting of clauses for sign language legislation.I have instructed my officials to continue this engagement with stakeholders, throughout the legislative process and beyond, to continuously improve access to services for our Deaf community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the criteria used to tender for the Rathlin ferry in (i) 2016; (ii) 2022; (iii) 2023; and (iv) 2024.
answer_text: My Department assisted by colleagues from Construction and Procurement Delivery awarded contracts for delivery of Rathlin Island Ferry service in 2016 and 2023. Both of these competitions followed the scope of the Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (as amended).Details of their award can be found by following the links below: -2016 Tender - ID 498825 Rathlin Island Ferry Service Provision -  https://ted.europa.eu/en/notice/-/detail/399320-2016)2023 Tender - DAC 128/22 DFI – Rathlin Island Ferry Service - https://www.find-tender.service.gov.uk/Notice/002833-2023

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the wastewater treatment works in North Antrim that currently have no capacity.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:There are currently six wastewater treatment works (WwTWs) located in North Antrim, with a PE (Population Equivalent) of 250 or greater, that NI Water  report ,currently have no capacity. These are:Armoy Bush WwTWClogh WwTWDervock WwTWMartinstown WwTWMosside Ballymoney WwTWStranocum WwTWFor any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the wastewater treatment works that have received an upgrade in North Antrim in the last five years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:In North Antrim, two wastewater treatment works have been upgraded in the last five years,  Ballintoy and Ballyvoy. Under the Rural Wastewater Improvement Programme, which targets investment at small rural treatment works to ensure ongoing compliance, work has also been completed in North Antrim in the last 5 years at Buckna, Gortereghy, Hillcrest Ballymoney, Lisnagunogue, Magherahoney and Racavan wastewater treatment works.In addition, the upgrade of the Grange wastewater treatment works is ongoing, with an expected completion date of Spring 2025.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the status of the implementation of the new waiver process for overpayments related to Welfare Supplementary Payments.
answer_text: The new Welfare Supplementary Payment overpayment waiver process was introduced on 30 September 2024. From this date, all Welfare Supplementary Payment overpayment notifications will advise customers of the waiver facility. NI Direct content has also been updated to reflect the new process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a further update on the recommendations in the Investing in Creative Delivery report.
answer_text: My statement of 3 July 2024 acknowledged the important work of the Culture, Arts and Heritage Taskforce and stated that the insights, observations and recommendations presented in “Investing in Creative Delivery” provide the basis for the policy development work to be undertaken for arts and for the built-heritage and the historic environment.  Work has now commenced on that policy development within the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme and remains ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has completed a Languages Strategies position paper for agreement on the next steps.
answer_text: I have been briefed on the position of the development of an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy, and an Irish Language Strategy. The Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG) is considering the draft strategies and associated action plans proposed by each strategies Co-Design Group. Once the work of the CDWG is complete, the draft strategies will issue to me for consideration prior to Executive consideration and public consultation. Alongside the work of the CDWG I am currently considering the next steps in the development of both strategies and associated action plans. I will then engage with Executive colleagues to ask them to consider and if content agree on those next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any progress made by the Languages Strategies Cross Departmental Working Group since it reconvened in April 2024.
answer_text: The Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG) is considering the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture and Irish Language draft strategies and action plans proposed by each strategy’s Co-Design Group. Once the work of the CDWG is complete, the draft strategies will issue to me for consideration prior to Executive consideration and public consultation. Alongside the work of the CDWG I am currently considering the next steps in the development of both strategies and associated action plans. I will then engage with Executive colleagues to ask them to consider and if content agree on those next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has completed its research on the condition of listed buildings in line with the Ministerial commitment to the Culture Arts and Heritage Taskforce.
answer_text: The research is complete.  A meeting took place on 24 September with the consultants to discuss final proofing and clarification of their report. The final document should be ready for publication by mid November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has finalised a timetable for the necessary stages to complete the various policies and related action plan for Culture and Creativity policies.
answer_text: Work has commenced on policy development within the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme. The current aim is for the first of the policies to be ready for public consultation in early 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has established contact arrangements and engagement processes for local councils to develop Culture and Creativity policies.
answer_text: As part of the ongoing policy development work within the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme my officials have established contact arrangements and are currently engaging with nominated representatives from each of the local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timeline in place to ensure that relevant legislation for social security, child maintenance, pensions, and welfare mitigations progresses through the legislative process by the March 2025 deadline.
answer_text: I can confirm that appropriate regulations in respect of social security, child maintenance and pensions have been made in the period 1 April – 30 September 2024. The Department will continue to bring forward legislation within timeframes informed by different policy requirements and in line with Northern Ireland legislative processes. These timeframes are often in place to retain parity with the Department for Work and Pensions.		I am also committed to the introduction of any new legislation required in respect of the existing Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes before 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has secured Ministerial agreement on any proposed amendments to the Discretionary Support Scheme.
answer_text: I am considering the recommendations from the Independent Review Panel for changes to Discretionary Support. These will be assessed in the climate of financial constraint with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.  The Committee for Communities will be notified of any proposed amendments in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timeline for proposed legislative amendments to address the findings from the internal and independent reviews of the Discretionary Support Scheme.
answer_text: I am considering the recommendations from the Independent Review Panel for changes to Discretionary Support. These will be assessed in the climate of financial constraint, with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.The Committee for Communities will be notified of any proposed amendments, including the timeline for any new legislation, in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any amendments being proposed to the Discretionary Support Scheme, including how they address the findings from the internal and independent reviews of the Discretionary Support Scheme.
answer_text: I am considering the recommendations from the Independent Review Panel for changes to Discretionary Support. These will be assessed in the climate of financial constraint, with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years. The Committee for Communities will be notified of any proposed amendments in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress to develop Partnership Agreements for all six Executive Committee Group arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: I can confirm that all six partnership agreements for the ALBs in the Engaged Communities Group in DfC are now complete and are available on the Departments website, the relevant ALB website and the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work that has been completed for the development and publication of a (i) Gender Equality Strategy; and (ii) Sexual Orientation Strategy.
answer_text: To date the development of the Gender Equality and Sexual Orientation Strategies has adopted the principle of co-design. This has involved a significant amount of engagement with internal and external stakeholders.  I have decided to take a phased approach to the development of the Social inclusion Strategies and the options for next steps and timings for development of the Sexual Orientation and Gender Equality Strategies will be dealt with in due course. Final decisions on the content, publication and implementation of the strategies will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timetable for the development and publication of a (i) Gender Equality Strategy; and (ii) Sexual Orientation Strategy.
answer_text: I have decided to take a phased approach to the development of Social inclusion Strategies and the options for next steps and timings for development of the Sexual Orientation and Gender Equality Strategies will be dealt with in due course. Whilst my Department is leading on the Executive Social Inclusion Strategies final decisions on their content, publication and implementation will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the main outcomes of the pre-consultation stakeholder engagement for the Fuel Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: The pre-consultation phase of the fuel poverty strategy comprised a series of workshops and focus groups held between April and July 2024. People from a wide range of backgrounds and organisations came together to inform the process, raise issues and concerns, bring forward new ideas, and highlight what has worked and what can be strengthened.Focus groups were held with vulnerable people and those who are most impacted by fuel poverty.  The discussions helped us to capture the real life experiences of vulnerable people and provided real insight into the impact of fuel poverty and the choices people make in their homes.The main outcomes that arose were: the lack of capacity of vulnerable people to seek support and the need to provide additional support for them to access help and advice.the need to enhance referral partnerships across Government to raise awareness and more effectively identify and support people in fuel poverty, the need to improve housing standards, particularly in the private rented sector andthe need to introduce a more ambitious fuel poverty intervention with appropriate standards and support for vulnerable households.  The Fuel Poverty strategy will launch its public consultation in Autumn 2024 and a summary of the events will be released along with this in a stakeholder engagement report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has (i) completed Year 2 of the People and Place Review sectoral engagement exercise; and (ii) prepared a draft strategy suitable for public consultation.
answer_text: The Review of the Executive’s People and Place strategy has progressed well with collaborative structures in place to facilitate engagement with a wide range of key stakeholders and other focus groups from across Northern Ireland.This work aims to produce a sound evidence-based approach that could be used to identify areas experiencing deprivation and on which a future funding programme to tackle deprivation could be based.  The Development of an outline Strategic Framework, informed by the views expressed through the stakeholder and sectoral engagement exercise has broadly concluded with a successful Strategic Insight Lab event in June.Subject to my consideration and that of my Executive colleagues, the Strategic Framework will be subject to a full public consultation exercise. Following a public consultation exercise, the planning assumption is that the new Strategic Framework will be completed during 2025, followed by a transition phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any interventions being developed to address food poverty and place-based deprivation.
answer_text: My Department has worked with local Councils on a co-design basis to roll out Social Supermarket models across Northern Ireland to assist those facing food poverty. The aim of Social Supermarkets is to offer a long term, sustainable, response by providing wraparound services that seek to address the underlying issues contributing to a household’s financial position alongside access to food.  The models continue to evolve and develop and in recognition of the level of need, and the challenges faced in providing a service across wide geographical areas, an increased budget of £2.75m has been allocated to support the continuation of this work in 2024/25.  My Department also leads on the People & Place strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal which aims to address place based depravation. Delivery is enabled through the Neighbourhood Renewal, Areas at Risk & Small Pockets of Deprivation Programmes with a total annual revenue budget of c£18m across the three programmes. A strategic Review of People and Place is currently being conducted which subject to my consideration and that of my Executive colleagues, will be subject to a full public consultation exercise with the planning assumption this will be completed during 2025 with the transition phase commencing thereafter.In the broader context, I remain committed to developing effective and, just as importantly, sustainable solutions to tackle poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland, through the implementation of a robust Anti-poverty Strategy.  The Anti-Poverty Strategy will aim to address poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty.While the Department for Communities is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy and final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement. I have written to Ministers of the other Departments asking them to nominate senior officials to re-establish a Cross Departmental Group to identify key actions that will have a meaningful and lasting impact on socio-economic disadvantage in our society. I am committed to working with all my Ministerial colleagues to ensure that the Anti-Poverty Strategy is taken forward in the most effective and expeditious way possible, taking account of departmental budget allocations and the need to develop a prioritised, sustainable and deliverable Strategy action plan.  I intend this work to be taken forward at pace, presenting a paper to the Executive early in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether the details of costs for new welfare mitigation payments were shared with the Executive by 31 August 2024; and (i) when these details will be published.
answer_text: Details of the estimated costs for additional mitigation measures, including those recommended in the Independent Review, will be shared with the Executive in due course. The information will also be shared with the Committee for Communities. There are currently no plans to publish this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department is putting in place to ensure there is a consistent delivery of direct payments for carers across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I would like to acknowledge the crucial role that carers play in our Health and Social Care System. Carers are essential partners in the delivery of care, and their wellbeing is integral to our considerations when assessing support packages.I want to assure the member that my Department is committed to ensuring that direct payments are delivered as needed for carers across all our Health and Social Care Trusts. It is important to recognise that direct payments are based on individual assessment of need via a Carer’s Assessment or Carer’s Conversation Wheel. This means that while we aim for consistency in respect of process, the outcomes and packages of care and support will necessarily vary according to each carer’s unique circumstances. A standardised set of criteria would therefore reduce flexibility and might not be in the best interests of individual carers. To address any concerns around disparity, I have asked my officials to provide an overview of the direct payments system to identify areas where improvements might be needed. This will help us to agree on the key areas of focus for future developments, subject of course to resource availability and funding. Operational responsibility for direct payments lies with individual Health and Social Care Trusts. They are tasked with implementing direct payments in line with the Carers and Direct Payments Act (Northern Ireland) 2002. I would also like to acknowledge the impact of rising costs on carers, but any increases in funding for direct payments will depend on the overall budget allocation for Health and Social Care. My Department will continue to review the funding position, but it remains subject to wider financial pressures and the prioritisation of services across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people that made new claims for Universal Credit in the last two years; and (ii) the number of those people that received their first payment on time.
answer_text: Universal Credit is a household benefit. In the period May 2022 to May 2024 there were 115,320 new claims to Universal Credit in which a household’s eligibility for Universal Credit was confirmed and their entitlement calculated. Official statistics are available to view at www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/universal-credit-statistics-may-2024 98% of these new claims received their first payment on time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14226/22-27, whether the remaining three Departments have provided their responses and nominated their respective officials.
answer_text: Since my response to AQW 14226/22-27, the remaining three Departments have provided their responses including details of their nominated respective officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the strategies he proposes to implement in order to address the identified areas for improvement listed on page 13 and page 14 of the First Report on the Operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: The cross-departmental Children and Young People’s Strategy Monitoring and Reporting Board is tasked with providing strategic direction in support of collaboration and co-operation on policy matters affecting children and young people. The Board will consider how all

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how the contribution of the culture, arts, and heritage sectors to departmental and executive policy outcomes will be (i) measured; and (ii) reported.
answer_text: The means by which the contribution of the culture, arts and heritage sectors to departmental and executive policy outcomes will be measured and reported will be developed as part of the process of policy-making.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any feedback or response has he received from Executive colleagues in relation to their support for a strategic approach to protect, support, and celebrate our culture, heritage, and the arts; and (ii) how this feedback will be communicated to the Assembly.
answer_text: Given the scale and scope of the work undertaken by the sectors associated with the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme I have invited Executive colleagues to nominate an official to join a cross-departmental group to consider those issues.  Since my Statement to the Assembly and my invitation to Executive colleagues on 3 July 2024, positive responses have been received from 5 Departments with details of nominated officials provided as requested.  I anticipate that similar responses from the remaining 3 Departments will be forthcoming shortly. In terms of engagement with the Assembly in relation to work under the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme, I intend to follow the established processes associated with the development of policy initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how will he ensure equality of access and opportunity across different socioeconomic groups, ages, and geographic locations when consulting with stakeholders on the strategic approach to protect, support, and celebrate our culture, heritage and the arts.
answer_text: The means to achieve the outcomes of the policies delivered under the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme will be developed as part of the process of policy-making. This process includes public consultation, in order that all those with an interest in the issue can have their voice heard and their views considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail in what capacity members of the Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy Taskforce will be involved in the new Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme.
answer_text: The formal remit of the Culture, Arts and Heritage Taskforce was discharged through the publication of “Investing in Creative Delivery”.   The individual experience and insights of members of that Task Force will continue to be of great value to the development of policy for the arts and heritage and will have an important place in the stakeholder engagement and consultation phases of that work.Given the role played by members of the Taskforce, my officials will continue to keep them informed on developments as work on the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how resources will be allocated to the new Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme to ensure continuity of, and build upon, the work and investment in the Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy Taskforce.
answer_text: A new Culture Policy Branch has been established within my Department’s Culture Division.  Its role is to support the implementation of the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme and it includes officials with experience of the work undertaken by the Culture, Arts and Heritage Taskforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the number of children living in relative and absolute poverty that have been affected by the two-child limit in the last five years; and (ii) for a breakdown of this as a percentage of the total number of children living in relative and absolute poverty.
answer_text: Official statistics on the number of children living in relative and absolute poverty in Northern Ireland are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). However, the FRS does not contain the data required to produce a robust estimate of the number and percentage of children living in relative or absolute poverty that have been affected by the two-child limit in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the five most common reasons for the rejection of applications Discretionary Support.
answer_text: Discretionary Support applications are governed by the Discretionary Support Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2016 which can be accessed via www.legislation.gov.uk/all?title=DISCRETIONARY%20SUPPORT%20REGULATIONS%202016 In respect of claims disallowed due to not meeting the criteria, the most common reasons include;Regulation 6 (evidence and information in connection with a claim) has not been satisfied. Regulation 9 (application withdrawn).  This is when an applicant chooses not to proceed with their application.Regulation 21 (2)(f) (in default).  This is where a customer has defaulted on a repayment plan of a previous DS loan and therefore cannot be considered for further awards until the default has been cleared. Regulation 10(3)(c)&(d) (maximum loans and grants paid) (rolling 12 months).  Customers cannot be awarded more than three Discretionary Support loans, one grant for living expenses and one grant for household items in a 12-month period, except in the case of a disaster.Regulation 10 (basic eligibility criteria) has not been satisfied.Further information relating to this scheme, including eligibility and how to apply, is available on nidirect: www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the findings and recommendations from the Investing in Creative Delivery report will be integrated into the new Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme.
answer_text: The scope of the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme extends beyond the remit of the Culture Arts and Heritage Taskforce and includes, for example, the development of policy in relation to museums and public libraries.  My statement of 3 July 2024 is clear in its acknowledgement of the important work of the Culture, Arts and Heritage Taskforce and in stating that the insights, observations and recommendations presented in “Investing in Creative Delivery” provide the basis for the policy development work to be undertaken for arts and for the built-heritage and the historic environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what measures are being taken to ensure that the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme (i) does not duplicate work already undertaken by the Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy Taskforce; and (ii) utilises the insights and data arising from the work of the Taskforce.
answer_text: My statement of 3 July 2024 is clear in its acknowledgement of the important work of the Culture, Arts and Heritage Taskforce and in stating that the insights, observations and recommendations presented in “Investing in Creative Delivery” provide the basis for the policy development work to be undertaken for arts and for the built-heritage and the historic environment.  	Far from seeking to duplicate the work of the taskforce, the work being undertaken by my officials will be built upon it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the number of households, with at least one parent in work, that are currently affected by the two-child limit; and (ii) for a breakdown of this as a percentage of all households in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: In February 2024, there were 5,260 households with at least one parent in work who were affected by the two-child limit. In February 2024, 3.8% of households in receipt of Universal Credit had at least one parent in work and were affected by the two-child limit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount spent by his Department on the uptake of the Universal Credit Contingency Fund in the last five years; and (ii) any associated underspend in the Universal Credit Contingency Fund budget over the same period.
answer_text: The Department publishes an annual report which details Discretionary Support performance and expenditure during each financial year. The annual reports can be accessed via the following website www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions There has been no underspend in the Universal Credit Contingency Fund budget in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the five most common reasons for the rejection of applications to the Universal Credit Contingency Fund in the last five years.
answer_text: The Universal Credit Contingency Fund Scheme is administered through the Department’s Discretionary Support Scheme. Applications are processed by Discretionary Support staff and are governed by the Discretionary Support Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2016 which can be accessed via www.legislation.gov.uk/all?title=DISCRETIONARY%20SUPPORT%20REGULATIONS%202016 The most common Universal Credit Contingency Fund claim disallowance reasons include;Regulation 10 (basic eligibility criteria) has not been satisfied.Regulation 6 (evidence and information in connection with a claim) has not been satisfied. Regulation 12 (grant eligibility) has not been satisfied.Regulation 13 (persons excluded from Discretionary Support). Excluded persons include prisoners or a person who is lawfully detained or is on temporary release, a resident in a residential care home, a nursing home or a hospital in-patient, a member of a religious order who is fully maintained by that order. Other regulations which include regulation 21 (principles of determination of the award) have not been satisfied. This takes account of whether the customers need can be met from other resources, the nature, extent and urgency of the need and taking account of the general guidance issued by the Department (list not exhaustive).Further information relating to this scheme, including eligibility and how to apply, is available on nidirect: www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there has been any engagement with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to the implementation of the Household Support Fund for families in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department provided advice to the Department for Work and Pensions when establishing their Household Support Fund which they closely aligned to our unique Northern Ireland Discretionary Support Scheme.To sustain Discretionary Support and ensure Northern Ireland citizens continue to receive this valuable support, my Department submitted a bid for an additional £12.7m Resource in 2024-25 to increase the Discretionary Support (DS) Grants historic Baseline funding of £13.7m to a total of £26.4m in 2024-25.  My Department was allocated a general allocation of £13m against total Resource bids of £128.8m and received no specific allocation for Discretionary Support.To help ensure this support remains available throughout this financial year, I have increased the initial Budget allocation for Discretionary Support Grants to £22m for 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures he plans to implement to support healthcare workers who are currently receiving Universal Credit, but who will lose their eligibility due to the upcoming lump sum pay award in August 2024.
answer_text: The amount of Universal Credit a person receives is likely to be reduced if their earnings increase, including the receipt of backpay. However, based on individual circumstances, a person may be eligible for a work allowance enabling them to earn a certain amount before their Universal Credit payments are reduced.  Claimants will also keep £0.45 of every £1.00 they earn over the work allowance. A person’s claim will not close if their earnings exceed their Universal Credit eligibility in one assessment period, claims will remain open and Universal Credit payments will resume in the next assessment period providing eligibility conditions continue to be met.  Further information on how Universal Credit treats earnings can be found at Universal Credit if you're employed | nidirect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will expedite any consideration of distributing the lump sum to healthcare workers in instalments, to ensure those entitled to Universal Credit are not disqualified from receiving it.
answer_text: My Department asked Regional Payroll within the Business Services Organisation (BSO) to work with Trust HR colleagues to formulate an implementation plan for all outstanding pay awards. Following their discussions over what was feasible, the resultant solution of splitting consolidated and non-consolidated payments was acknowledged as the best way of ensuring all staff would at least receive their substantive pay award in the June pay run. Non-consolidated payments will follow in August. The alternative to this would have been to delay implementation of all elements of the awards, for all staff, until September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is a mechanism in his Department to write to residents who live in the vicinity of Glenshesk Road, Ballycastle, to keep them informed of the investigation in relation to the recent landslide and subsequent road closure.
answer_text: My Department has arranged for a detailed geotechnical investigation to fully assess the extent of the damage.  When this work is complete, my officials will be able to progress the design and, subject to the necessary approvals, construction of remedial works.   As this is a complex issue it is likely to take some time to complete the investigation and develop and implement an appropriate solution. As such, it is not currently possible to advise when the road will reopen.My Department will continue to work at pace to reopen this road as quickly as possible and is making arrangements to keep the public informed of any updates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there will be consultation with organisations working with those in receipt of Universal Credit in relation to the name change of the Universal Credit Contingency Fund.
answer_text: My Department will engage with relevant stakeholders prior to any implementation of a change in name to the Universal Credit Contingency Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Participatory Budgeting pilot scheme delivered by his Department; and (ii) whether the scheme will be repeated.
answer_text: There is no pilot scheme for participatory budgeting delivered by DfC, however the Department does endorse this method of public engagement which continues to be used effectively by community planning partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the recently announced £1.45 million increase in funding for his Department's 2024-25 budget will be allocated to the voluntary and community sector.
answer_text: Final confirmation of budget allocations remains subject to the outcome of consultation on the equality impacts of my 2024/25 spending proposals.  I can confirm that community and voluntary sector groups with an existing grant funding relationship with my department have been informed to plan the remainder of the 2024/25 financial year based on an uplift of 5% on their 2023/24 opening funding allocation. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail Tourism NI's (i) opening budget; and (ii) final closing budget for the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s opening budget was £19.824m and final closing budget was £24.809m for the 2023-24 financial year.

answer_text: AQW 12770 22-27 was answered on 08 July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 12349/22-27.
answer_text: The answer to AQW 12349/22-27 was issued on 19 June 2024.The delay in responding was due to the time taken to obtain and coordinate information on the status of the future plans for the disposal of these properties, which are owned by the Northern Regional College and the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a detailed timetable for completing work on the anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland through the implementation of a robust Anti-poverty Strategy.While initial drafting of the Strategy has taken place, I will be considering next steps taking account of departmental budget allocations and the need to develop a prioritised, sustainable and deliverable Strategy action plan.  	Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be at the Executive’s discretion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the first report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a timeline detailing the work completed in the development of an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: Initial work on development of the Anti-Poverty Strategy began in Autumn 2020 with the agreement of the Executive to a Co-design process. Following this, in late 2020, an Expert Advisory Panel, Co-design Group and Cross Departmental Working Group were established to take this work forward.The Expert Advisory Panel prepared a report with detailed recommendations and shared this the Department. This report was then published on the Department’s website in March 2021. During this period the Cross Departmental group, consisting of officials from across the NICS also met on a monthly basis to discuss work ongoing in this area.The Co-design Group, made up of members of the voluntary and community sectors, undertook discussions in relation to the Expert Panel’s recommendations during 2021, and provided the Department with a written position paper in February 2022. An initial draft of a Strategy was presented to the then Minister in October 2022. Addressing poverty in all its forms has been a key priority for me since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland through the implementation of a robust Anti-poverty Strategy.	I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be at the Executive’s discretion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the support his Department provides to farmers who are in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: My Department delivers Universal Credit from a network of 35 locally based Jobs and Benefit offices and through its regionally based service centres. My Universal Credit work coaches and case managers provide focused, bespoke face the face, telephony, and online support to help farmers make and maintain their Universal Credit claim.  This will include explaining the different terminology, supporting them to report their income and expenses and telling them about the different in work allowances that Universal Credit provides.These include the work allowance, how the earnings taper will reduce the impact of the wages they receive, the childcare element that pays up to 85% of childcare and my Departments Adviser Discretion Fund.  This fund provides a non-repayable grant of up to £1500, to help unemployed and self-employed people overcome the financial barriers to starting or returning to work, including the cost of upfront childcare. Further information on self-employment can be found online at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-if-youre-self-employed Recognising the particular challenges faced by the farming community, my senior officials met with representatives from the Ulster Farmer’s Union to gain a better understanding of the difficulties they will face reporting their income and expenses monthly.  Importantly they offered to work with the Ulster Farmer’s Union to help ease the transition from tax credits to Universal Credit and they stand ready with this offer.  Separately my officials have been working with the Advice Sector to increase communication and awareness of the support we offer, answer queries and to further increase our awareness of the additional support my staff working in Universal Credit need to provide for farmers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps he will take to make it more accessible for students to apply to universities in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: It is a priority for me to improve student mobility across Ireland.  Prior to his becoming Taoiseach, I had a positive discussion with Simon Harris TD in his then capacity as the responsible Minister on steps we can take to reduce barriers across the island.I have reached out to my counterpart, Minister Patrick O’Donovan TD, with a view to continuing that dialogue.  I intend to explore ways we can remove barriers to cross-border study as part of that engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps he will take to make it more accessible for students to apply to universities in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: It is a priority for me to improve student mobility across Ireland.
