answer_text: I will be submitting the first report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to the Executive at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average length of time families with children have been placed in non-standard temporary accommodation before being moved into permanent accommodation in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not record the reason for which placements have been closed and therefore, is unable to identify the number of cases which have moved directly from non-standard temporary accommodation to permanent accommodation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024 was published on 13 June and contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024.Table 3.5 provides detail on current placements in temporary accommodation by banded length of stay and accommodation type.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of families with children placed in non-standard temporary accommodation; and (b) the number of those families where domestic abuse was a factor in their housing application, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024, published on 13 June, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024 Table 2.1 details households accepted as homeless by reason. Table 3.3 details children in temporary accommodation by type of accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he intends to promote the Universal Credit Contingency Fund short-term living expenses grant, as per the suggestion of the Cliff Edge Coalition.
answer_text: My Department has taken several steps to promote availability of the Universal Credit Contingency Fund.The first journal message received by those making a new claim to Universal Credit provides details of the help available if they are facing any financial difficulty when waiting on their first payment. This message includes details of the Universal Credit Contingency Fund and a link to the application process. There is a dedicated Finance Support campaign page on the nidirect website, developed using stakeholder feedback, which enables users to obtain information on all financial help available, including the Universal Credit Contingency Fund. Information can be found at: www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-supportThe Universal Credit Contingency Fund continues to feature within the Department’s digital advertising, most recently during May and June, with direct links to the nidirect website for more information. My Department also promotes the availability of the Universal Credit Contingency Fund through its close links with advice sector colleagues and has cascaded awareness of the scheme though its wide range of Neighbourhood Renewal partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to a name change for the Universal Credit Contingency Fund short-term living expenses grant, as per the recommendations of the Independent Review of Welfare Mitigations.
answer_text: The Independent Advisory Panel's Report is currently under review with a refresh of the estimated costs for the various recommendations being finalised.  I have asked officials, subject to affordability, to expedite the arrangements for changing the name of the Universal Credit Contingency Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department will finalise the work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations set out in the Independent Advisory Panel Report on Mitigations, including mitigating the Universal Credit two-child limit.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Advisory Panel’s Review to help inform decisions on the way forward.  It is anticipated that this work will be completed in the coming weeks.Once this work to refresh the estimated costs is complete I will share further details with the Executive and the Committee for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department will produce a plan and timeline for the implementation of measures which will fully mitigate the impact of the two-child limit on families.
answer_text: Officials are currently finalising work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Independent Advisory Panel Report into the future of welfare mitigations, including mitigation for the two-child limit.  Any future mitigation package will need to be considered and agreed by the Executive, and assessed in the climate of financial constraint with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5976/22-27, to detail (i) whether the final stage of the design has now completed; and (ii) the date for commencement of the construction of Slemish College, Ballymena.
answer_text: The project for Slemish College is in the latter stages of design and this is anticipated to be completed in summer 2024. The project will proceed to construction when affordable within the wider education budget context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 10538/22-27, whether officials have engaged with Department of Finance counterparts and come to a conclusion as to whether your Department will be updating the guidance on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015, to support or promote the discretionary power on pooling and sharing of resources outlined in Section 4.
answer_text: Engagement with Department of Finance officials on this issue is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the detailed geotechnical investigation to assess the extent of the damage and works required to repair Glenshesk Road, Ballycastle, has commenced; and (ii) to detail a timeframe for the completion of this investigation.
answer_text: Work is currently underway to develop a ground investigation specification, which will be finalised within the coming weeks.  Once this is completed and certified, physical ground investigation works will be undertaken on site allowing the preparation of a ground investigation report.  The ground investigation report is anticipated to be drafted by the end of August 2024.This ground investigation report will then inform the detailed design process and the subsequent remedial construction work.  This is a complex issue that will require significant engineering consideration to allow a safe and feasible solution to be developed. However, I am very mindful of the impact this road closure is having on the local community and I have asked my officials to work at pace and bring forward an accelerated programme for the delivery of a proposed solution.

To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 9960/22-27.

answer_text: The first report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 has not yet been presented to the Executive. Publication will be a matter for the Executive to consider in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8559/22-27, when the Youth Homelessness Action Plan will be published.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“It is intended that the Youth Homelessness Action Plan will be published shortly after the ongoing pre-election period associated with the Westminster election.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place for the former Portrush Technical School building, located at Dunluce Avenue, Portrush.
answer_text: The former Portrush Technical School is owned by the Northern Regional College (NRC). However, it is adjoined to the vacant former Portrush Youth Centre, which is owned by the Education Authority (EA).A joint disposal of both buildings is planned to be undertaken in line with Land and Property Services guidance.The EA is currently finalising legal agreements with the NRC in anticipation of the disposal commencing in the 2024/25 Financial Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what progress his Department has made to implement the recommendations of the Investing in Creative Delivery report, since it was published in July 2023.

To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the findings of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care by Professor Ray Jones, what actions he will take to address the issue of children living in poverty, in order to avoid children being placed on the child protection register or becoming looked after children.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities, including children and our young people, right across Northern Ireland.The Anti-Poverty Strategy will aim to address the root causes of poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and inequality and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty. I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and addressing the issues faced by children and young people remains a key focus of this ongoing work.Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement and it is anticipated that child poverty will be included as part of the broader Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the refurbishment of Ballymoney library; and (ii) to detail the timeline for completion.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Libraries NII have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The project being delivered by Libraries NI to refurbish and improve the energy efficiency of the Ballymoney library facility is progressing at pace with construction works scheduled to complete mid-May 2024. Commissioning of systems, provision of furniture, equipment and library stock will then take place, and it is planned that the facility will reopen to the public early in June 2024”.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Libraries NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what he is doing to explore how to more effectively use the discretionary power outlined in Section 4 and Section 8 of the Children’s Services Co-operation (Northern Ireland) Act (2015), relating to the sharing of resources and the pooling of funds cross-departmentally, to increase and expand existing provisions often provided by the voluntary and community sector in early intervention.

The Primary purpose of CYPSP is to establish and maintain an agreed basis for collaboration in the delivery of children’s services in order to address the eight outcomes of wellbeing as set out in the N.I. Children and Young People’s Strategy (2019-2029), in line with the Programme for Government (2016-2021) and the Children’s Services Co-operation (Northern Ireland) Act (2015).The CYPSP structure includes 5 Multi agency Outcomes groups and 24Locality Planning Groups who work together to develop localised multiagency plans to support the mobilisation of resources to meet locallyidentified needs.Below is a list of some of the examples of community /voluntary and statutory activity which seeks to expand existing Early intervention provision for children and families:CYPSP Youth Wellness Web - Early intervention mental health andemotional wellbeing digital platform designed by young people for young people – and jointly resourced by Department of Health, (DOH), Health and Social care Trusts (HSCTs), Education Authority (EA); Public Health Agency (PHA); Armagh and Banbridge City Council (ABC); Mid Ulster Council; NI Youth Forum; Bolster Community; Include Youth; Youth@CLC.( Childrens Law Centre)CYPSP Translation Hub - Early Intervention advice and signpostingDigital platform for Minority Ethnic Communities with limited EnglishContributors include Department of Health; Health and Social Care Trusts; Education Authority; Public Health Agency; Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI); Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE); and a wide range of community and voluntary sector organisations.CYPSP Your Journey through disability guide - Early inventionssupport, advice and signposting for Parents of Children with Disability –Contributors: Department of Health; question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what he is doing to explore how to more effectively use the discretionary power outlined in Section 4 and Section 8 of the Children’s Services Co-operation (Northern Ireland) Act (2015), relating to the sharing of resources and the pooling of funds cross-departmentally, to increase and expand existing provisions often provided by the voluntary and community sector in early intervention.
answer_text: Guidance on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 (CSCA) which includes advice on the sharing of resources and pooling of funds is in place. It can be accessed at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-childrens-services-co-operation-act-northern-ireland-2015 My officials are engaging with Department of Finance counterparts to consider if there is merit in reviewing the guidance referred to, thereby providing an opportunity to consider how it might be updated to support or promote the discretionary power on pooling and sharing of resources outlined in Section 4 of the CSCA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has plans to use the developer bond to complete the West Strand Road, Portrush, given the difficulties currently faced in its completion.
answer_text: West Strand Road, Portrush remains unadopted as the developer has not completed the streets. There are difficulties associated with the bond of surety, the road layout, and the landowner of the road.  The bond agreement does not specify a particular time period for the developer to complete, however, my Department will continue to encourage the developer to bring the road to an adoptable standard.The Department has no current plans to call in the bond, however, may consider enforcement to complete pre-adoption repairs if the developer defaults in his responsibility. The Department is not obliged under The Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980 to serve notice or carry out any enforcement actions necessary to bring a development up to an adoptable standard, whether the developer is trading or dissolved. The powers to serve notice and undertake works are discretionary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services by Professor Ray Jones, whether there has been any collaboration, or planned collaboration, with the Minister for Communities to look at expanding early prevention and support services for children leaving care and to enable better links with the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group focusing on early help in community settings.
answer_text: Professor Ray Jones engaged with the Department for Communities (DfC) during the Review and my Department engaged with DfC on the the public consultation on the Review recommendations. There is ongoing collaboration between my Department and DfC. A cross-departmental plan has been developed to implement the recommendations of a September 2023 report of a joint review of jointly commissioned supported accommodation arrangements for children and young people aged 16+ to address the housing needs of care leavers and young people who are homeless. My officials are also fully engaged in a programme of work, being led by NIHE, with Homewards NI, to address the particular vulnerabilities of young people leaving care which can make them at greater risk of homelessness than their non-care experienced peers. HSC Trusts collaborate with NIHE in exercising duties under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 to support young people in care as they prepare to transition to independent living; this includes an assessment of their needs including accommodation.  There are 29 Family Support Hubs across Northern Ireland providing access to early intervention services; one of which is funded by DfC.  The services provided by the over 600 Hub member organisations are funded by a range of organisations including DfC, the Department of Education, the Public Health Agency and a range of other organisations. The Hubs provide a range of early supports to families including early help services to support mental health and emotional wellbeing. In 2023, the PHA, in collaboration with the Department of Health, published a 3-year ‘Early Intervention and Prevention Plan’ and has established a cross-sectoral Steering Group to oversee implementation of the actions. This Steering Group includes representation from the DfC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work he is (i) undertaking; and (ii) planning to undertake with the Minister for Communities to ensure a regionally equitable approach is taken to planning for those transitioning from care to independent living.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have duties under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and associated regulations to support children in care as they prepare to transition to independent living.  These duties include assessing the needs of all relevant children to determine what advice, assistance and support it would be appropriate to provide the child in preparation for leaving care, and after the young person has left care.  The Trust must then prepare a pathway plan for the child and keep this under review.  The assessment of the child’s needs must take account of the need for accommodation, and the pathway plan must include details of the accommodation arrangements for the child or young person.  As required by the Children Order, HSC Trusts cooperate with NIHE in the exercise of these functions.The then HSC Board also worked with NIHE Supporting People to undertake a joint review of jointly commissioned supported accommodation arrangements for children and young people aged 16+, with the review published in September 2023. A cross-departmental plan has been developed to implement the recommendations of that review to address the housing needs of care leavers and young homeless. My officials are also fully engaged in a programme of work, being led by NIHE, with Homewards NI, to address the particular vulnerabilities of young people leaving care which make them at greater risk of homelessness than their non-care experienced peers. In terms of children with disabilities and/or Special Educational Needs, there is a significant body of work underway and planned to address Tranisitons. My officials jointly chair a cross-departmental working group with the Department for Education to review and improve transitions for school leaver, including active participation by the Department for Communities and Department for Economy. It is also recognised that HSC transitions should be improved through the lens of regional consistency and multi-agency working. In November 2023, my officials established a HSC sub-group on Transitions, which has produced a draft regional protocol for Trusts. A workshop to finalise the protocol is planned for May 2024, with further work planned to engage housing colleagues. My officials have established a supported living workstream as part of the Social Care Collaborative Forum, to improve collaboration between Health, Communities and Housing and enable greater opportunities for independent living.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work his Department is (i) undertaking; and (ii) planning to undertake to provide suitable and affordable accommodation for those transitioning from care to independent living.
answer_text: The Department funds the Supporting People Programme, protecting its annual budget of £72.8 million, for the commissioning by the NIHE of the provision of housing support services to enable vulnerable people to live as independently as possible in the community.  The governance and oversight of the commissioned services sits with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. The NI Executive has agreed the 2024/25 Budget, now I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s settlement against key priorities. Commissioning of suitable and affordable accommodation is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought information from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows: ‘The Supporting People (SP) Programme provides funding for services to support Young People aged 16 – 21 years, who are transitioning from care and those who are young homeless presenters, who may or may not be care experienced.  A continuum of housing support for Young People is funded to ensure positive outcomes as they transition on their pathway to independent living. This includes Jointly Commissioned Supported Accommodation, STAY Projects, Shared Tenancies and Floating/Outreach Support provision which provides support to attain and maintain tenancies, preventing homelessness and providing pathways into independent living.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work he is (i) undertaking; and (ii) planning to undertake with the Minister of Health to ensure a regionally equitable approach is taken to planning for those transitioning from care to independent living.
answer_text: The Department funds the Supporting People Programme, protecting its annual budget of £72.8 million, for the commissioning by the NIHE of the provision of housing support services to enable vulnerable people to live as independently as possible in the community.  The governance and oversight of the commissioned services sits with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. The Programme commissions housing-related support services to vulnerable people, including those transitioning from care, by enabling them to have greater independence. The Department of Health sit on the Supporting People Strategic Advisory Board, chaired by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, which advises the NIHE Board of the strategic funding priorities of the Supporting People Programme in line with the overarching policy, operational strategy, budget allocation and relevant governance arrangements. The Strategic Advisory Board should ensure that these priorities are reflected consistently and in a standardised manner in the decisions relating to the grant funding and planning structures of the SP Programme. The Strategic Advisory Board provides assurance that processes and practices are fair, transparent and equitable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to fully mitigate the five-week wait in Universal Credit for care experienced young people who are leaving care.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to fully mitigate the five-week wait for Universal Credit for young people who are leaving care.Care leavers can prepare their claim to Universal Credit up to 28 days before and including their 18th birthday, although the claim details must not be submitted until their 18th birthday. This helps to ensure that support is in place as early as possible.There is a range of support available for those people facing financial difficulty while waiting for their first full Universal Credit payment, including the Universal Credit Contingency Fund, a non-repayable grant that is only available in Northern Ireland.New claims advances of up to 100% of potential Universal Credit entitlement are also available if a person needs support. The repayment period for new claim advances is 24 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) will take to implement Recommendation 47 of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care by Professor Ray Jones.
answer_text: As the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care does not fall within the remit of my Department, I have not taken steps to implement Recommendation 47 of the Review. However, in the spirit of cross departmental collaboration, I will work with my Executive colleagues to progress any actions relevant to my Department in relation to the implementation of this Recommendation.My department leads for the Executive on the strategic relationship with the Voluntary and Community Sector, through facilitating and co-chairing the Joint Forum between all of Government, including local government and the Sector.  The Joint Forum has developed a successor to the 2011 Concordat, which will take the form of a fresh Partnership Agreement, seeking a commitment from all departments and their agencies to improved practices; including engagement, commissioning and funding approaches that are fair and sustainable, and which recognise the importance and value of the community sector. I expect that work to be completed before the Summer Recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) will take to implement Recommendation 47 of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care by Professor Ray Jones.
answer_text: My officials are in the process of establishing a further Workstream of the Children’s Social Care Strategic Reform Programme to, among other things, progress recommendation 47. Terms of Reference for the Workstream will be developed jointly with the Re-imagine Children's Collective and we will ensure that the new Workstream connects fully with other strands of work under the Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the research report, being carried out by a consortium of LGBT organisations, into the banning of conversion therapies.
answer_text: The previous Minister agreed to provide funding to a consortium of organisations from the LGBT sector to work in partnership with academics from Ulster University and Queen’s University Belfast to carry out research into conversion practices in Northern Ireland. I understand that the research project is near completion and will be published in the coming months.  I will carefully consider the results of this research once available.Considerable evidence and data have been gathered by my officials, in addition to this research; and close contact has been maintained with colleagues from other jurisdictions to learn from their experiences. I am determined that all those who have an interest in this issue can have their views heard and further engagement with stakeholders will be required as the development of policy on this issue is progressed.I will be engaging with my officials over the coming weeks to understand work completed to date and to consider the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) will take to work with the community and voluntary sector as a result of the Consultation on Voluntary and Community Sector Infrastructure Support and fair funding principles.
answer_text: My department has engaged extensively and worked closely with the Sector to shape policy proposals for future support, through a dedicated representative Policy Reference Group, a series of stratified pre-consultation engagement sessions and subsequently through the 12 week consultation process itself.Continued working with the sector will be an integral part of the next phase of development, which will see implementation of new infrastructure support arrangements. Identifying key delivery partners in the Sector will be important to ensure effective future funding support.  My department’s shared facilitation of the Joint Forum between government and the sector will bring forward proposals on shared values, ways of working and fair funding principles before the summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans his Department has to address the issue of rural youth homelessness.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, as such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 supports the Housing Executive in taking a local approach to service delivery.  Homelessness Local Area Groups play a key role in the delivery of the aims and objectives of the Homelessness Strategy at a local level and this in turn assists the Housing Executive in addressing the issue of rural youth homelessness.  Local Action Plans have been developed using homelessness data and evidence from members to devise responses to the local issues identified, including rural youth homelessness where appropriate.  Key members for most groups include housing, health, justice and support partners working together. Building on the work of the Local Area Groups in addressing rural youth homelessness and supporting the delivery of the Homelessness Strategy, the Housing Executive has developed a Youth Homelessness Action Plan. The Youth Homelessness Action Plan aims to provide a coordinated response to ensure the Housing Executive addresses the challenges posed by youth homelessness with a focus on prevention, support and accommodation options.  Addressing homelessness and housing need amongst young people and families with children is a distinct priority for the Housing Executive and it is wholly committed to achieving better outcomes for these cohorts.  Subject to approval, the Youth Homelessness Action Plan will be published in May 2024.  The Youth Homelessness Action Plan will consider any needs identified by the Local Area Groups in respect of youth homelessness and the Housing Executive will seek to develop actions accordingly.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) will take to engage with the Reimagine Collective, in light of their policy paper on Reimagining Social Care Services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities, including children and young people, right across Northern Ireland.Listening to and considering the views of stakeholders will be essential to this work, including, for example, the Reimagine Collective’s policy paper on Reimagining Social Care Services in Northern Ireland. I should point out, however, that any future welfare mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years. My focus will be on working with my Executive colleagues, and meeting with sectoral delivery partners and key stakeholders, to develop a sustainable, deliverable Anti-Poverty Strategy which addresses the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and addressing the issues faced by children and young people remains a key focus of this ongoing work. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department has put in place to adhere to the Children's Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: My officials are fully engaged in the mechanisms established and led by the Department of Education on behalf of the Executive to meet the requirement of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to adopt a children and young people’s strategy.  This includes the Children and Young People’s Strategy Monitoring and Reporting Board established to provide collaborative leadership, oversight and cross-departmental strategic direction on all policy matters affecting children and young people.  It will monitor progress against commitments in the Executive’s 2020-2030 Children and Young People’s Strategy identifying areas for prioritisation and driving greater levels of co-operation across government. There are several examples of initiatives being taken forward by my Department collaboratively with other departments, bodies and voluntary organisations in keeping with the requirements of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to work together to improve the wellbeing of children and young people.These include the ‘Life Deserved Strategy’ for Looked After Children developed with the Department of Education together with other stakeholders. Both Departments and their arm's length bodies are continuing to cooperate on the implementation of the Strategy's commitments to action, including through a review of care planning and looked after child review arrangements, the development of a revised Personal Education Planning process for primary aged children who are looked after, and the pooling of resources to support the implementation of the Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  The ‘Life Deserved’ Strategy also introduced the ‘Corporate Family’ approach in recognition of the need for a multi-agency response to improve outcomes for care-experienced young people or those at risk of coming into care.   The joint Regional Care and Justice Campus Programme has led to the sharing of services and the pooling of funds to establish a single dedicated multi-disciplinary team providing enhanced therapeutic care across Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre and Lakewood Regional Secure Care Centre.   There is close co-operation between health and education in planning and improving provision for children with Special Educational Needs (SEN).  This includes the collation of data by health professionals to inform long-term planning for provision in education, the development and review of cross-departmental pathways and the creation of SEN coordinator posts and data managers within HSCTs, which have significantly improved response times to statutory assessment requests from the Education Authority.   Health and education have jointly funded SEN Partnership Lead to enhance participation and relationships of children and families, local communities, and voluntary sector partners in the delivery and planning of services. Officials from my Department and the Department of Education co-chair a working group alongsid

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6860/22-27, (i) when he expects to make a decision on funding for the Core Grant Scheme; (ii) what options he is considering in relation to funding for the Core Grant Scheme; and (iii) for details of his Department's Equality Impact Assessment regarding the options for the Core Grant Scheme.
answer_text: I will be making a decision on the Core Grant Scheme in the coming weeks. My Department’s budget assessment has already made it clear that the challenging allocation for Health this year will require difficult decisions to be taken, including in relation to funding for the Community and Voluntary Sector.Early responses to the Department’s Equality Impact Assessment will be considered in making budget decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the funding opportunities for organisations who want to use a street-based or detached youth work methodology.
answer_text: Feedback from organisations within the voluntary youth sector indicated that the funding process was too prescriptive. Subsequently, since 2023-24, funding opportunity titles no longer state ‘Detached Work’ or ‘Street Based Youth Work’ as a required methodology.  Instead, the funding opportunity outlines the assessed need and invites applicants to set out how they will address an identified assessed need when making their application, which may or may not include detached youth work.  There are 95 active Local Area Funding awards continuing into 2024-25. Just over half (53%) of these awards include funding for at least one flexible youth work session per week which may be used to deliver detached youth work, should the award holder believe this is a priority use of the resource in line with assessed need at the local level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what progress has been made on legislation to add Universal Credit to the eligible benefits for financial support for healthcare.
answer_text: Preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004. The focus of work is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department for Communities (DfC).The Department, in consultation with DfC, has implemented an interim solution to help ensure that people do not lose out on their entitlement. However, this is no longer an automatic system. It is paper based, and individuals must apply for an annual certificate. This has obvious implications for some of our most vulnerable people. While the Department is currently liaising with officials in DfC to explore options for streamline this process, it is clear that there is an urgent need to update our legislation to reinstate automatic passporting to those eligible.The Department will seek to maintain, as closely as possible, the caseload of recipients who are eligible for HwHC, balancing the continued protection of the health of members of society who need additional support without adding pressures on HSC budgets. My Department is currently liaising with other NI departments and colleagues in GB. This will inform the decision on setting the income threshold for UC becoming an automated passport benefit. A short public consultation will be conducted in the coming months on proposed changes to our legislation in relation to UC, with the associated legislative process commencing in due course thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many households in the private rented sector are in receipt of Local Housing Allowance (LHA), through (a) universal credit housing costs; and (b) housing benefit; (ii) how many of those households rents exceed the LHA; and (iii) to detail the median average gap between rent and the LHA, for each broad rental market area, for the most recent period for which data is available.
answer_text: (i) In November 2023, there were 36,670 households in the private rented sector in receipt of Local Housing Allowance (LHA), through Universal Credit housing costs and 22,740 claimants in the private rented sector in receipt of LHA, through housing benefit.(ii) Of the 36,670 households in receipt of LHA through Universal Credit in November 2023, 32,020 had rents that exceeded their LHA entitlement.Of the 22,740 claimants in receipt of LHA through Housing Benefit in November 2023, 18,130 had rents that exceeded their LHA entitlement.(iii)  The median gap between rent and LHA entitlement in November 2023 for each broad rental market area is shown below for both Universal Credit households and Housing Benefit claimants.LHA area*Median Monthly difference (HB)Median Monthly difference (UC)Belfast BRMA£87£126Lough Neagh lower BRMA£102£134lough Neagh Upper BRMA£92£132North BRMA£85£114North West BRMA£91£114South BRMA£112£139South East BRMA£105£133South West BRMA£103£132NI£97£129Data Source: NIHE Housing Benefit Data and Universal Credit Full service system (UCFS) - November 2023Note that due to retrospection of UCFS data UC figures are subject to change.In producing the geographical analysis, records were attributed to an LHA area on the basis of their postcode. Not all records can be correctly allocated using this method and some cannot be allocated at all.All claimant / household figures are rounded to the nearest ten.Note that the rate of LHA used to calculate Housing Benefit/Universal Credit housing costs award depends on the area where the customer chooses to live, who lives with them and the number of bedrooms they need.  This may have an impact on the median monthly difference as some customers may be under-occupying their property and will therefore have a larger shortfall between their eligible rent used to calculate their Housing Benefit/Universal Credit housing costs award and the contractual rent charged by their landlord.The median figures above are an average across all household size requirements (number of bedrooms needed) and so the median difference will be affected by the characteristics of the cohort, including household size, within each BRMA. This should also be considered when drawing comparisons between UC and HB due to the differences in their caseload characteristics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether an equality impact screening exercise was conducted prior to the decision not to extend the legislation that allowed for hybrid meeting arrangements for local government.
answer_text: An equality impact screening exercise was not conducted prior to my decision not to extend the legislation allowing for remote/hybrid meeting arrangements for councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide any equality screening assessments carried out regarding the decision not to proceed with an extension to the legislation that allowed for hybrid meeting arrangements for local government.
answer_text: An equality impact screening exercise was not conducted prior to my decision not to extend the legislation allowing for remote/hybrid meeting arrangements for councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to put standalone legislation in place to allow for hybrid working arrangements in local government.
answer_text: I am committed to giving councils the flexibility to hold meetings by remote or hybrid means. Standalone legislation already exists to allow for hybrid working arrangements. I will be bringing forward regulations, within as quick a timeframe as possible, using the powers in the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (NI) 2021, and this work is ongoing at pace.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his decision not to extend legislative provisions for councils to operate remote or hybrid meetings.
answer_text: There is no longer justification under the Coronavirus Act 2020 to provide councils with powers to hold remote or hybrid meetings.  I have asked officials to bring forward regulations, as soon as possible, to give councils the flexibility to hold meetings by remote or hybrid means using enabling powers in the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (NI) 2021.  I consider this to be the only appropriate vehicle to provide provision for remote/hybrid meetings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the implementation of the recommendations of the Truth Recovery Design Panel's report on Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made on the implementation of the 5 key recommendations including establishing a Victim-Survivor Consultation Forum which has met on 29 occasions. A non-statutory 10-person Independent Panel was appointed in April 2023 to take forward the first part of the investigation and PRONI has commenced cataloguing and digitising 4,500 institutional records.In addition, to date a specialist and dedicated support service has assisted 300 people   providing a critical element to support Victims and Survivors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to publish the findings of the Education and Training Inspectorate's review of youth service funding.
answer_text: The Education & Training Inspectorate (ETI) were commissioned to provide advice to the Department on the impact of the Education Authority’s (EA) funding scheme on addressing children and young people’s assessed needs. While the final policy advice has not yet been received, ETI has presented initial findings to officials, but as this advice was commissioned to inform policy development it was not designed to be released as a full report.That said it has been agreed that a summary of the findings and key messages will be disseminated, and my officials have committed to provide that summary to the youth sector. My officials will work with the EA and voluntary youth sector to address the findings and recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the implementation of the recommendations contained within the Investing in Creative Delivery report.
answer_text: The report by the Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy Taskforce – Investing in Creative Delivery – is informing the development of a Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy. My officials are progressing this, engaging with local and central government prior to relevant engagement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust area relating to (i) suicide; and (ii) drug-related causes, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. The most recent published data for suicide, and drug-related deaths is available up to 2022. The information requested for (i) was published in the release Suicide statistics 2022 tables. Table 7 specifically contains the number of suicides registered each year in Northern Ireland by Health and Social Care Trust.The information requested for (ii) was published in the release Drug-Related and Drug Misuse Deaths 2012-2022 tables. Table 8a specifically contains the number of drug-related deaths registered each year by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools in North Antrim that are awaiting the beginning or completion of capital projects, including new builds; (ii) the progress, status and expected completion date of each of these capital build projects; and (iii) the current ranking for the prioritisation of the completion of outstanding capital works for schools in North Antrim.
answer_text: Five schools in North Antrim have been announced under the Major Capital Works Programme, two schools have been announced under the Fresh Start Agreement and two schools have been announced under the School Enhancement Programme. Twenty-seven schools have minor works schemes which are in various stages of planning. The status of these capital projects is as follows:Major Capital ProgrammeMary Queen of Peace Primary School was announced to advance in planning in January 2013. Planning approval for the new school build was granted in April 2021. Outline agreement for land purchase has very recently been agreed, however acquisition of the site cannot be progressed at this time due to the current capital budget position. Procurement of the Building Contractor has also been paused at this time again due to the current capital budget position.Cullybackey College was announced to advance in planning in June 2014.An Integrated Consultant Team has been appointed for the project and the initial technical feasibility report to explore options to provide replacement accommodation has been completed. The Education Authority has also completed a Business Case, which is currently with Departmental economists for review, and it is anticipated that the Business Case will be submitted to the Department of Finance for consideration in the coming weeks. Dunclug High School was announced to advance in planning in June 2014. This project was at an advanced stage of design (RIBA Stage 4 -Technical Design) when the appointed Integrated Supply Team formally withdrew from  their contract on 23 January 2022. A decision was taken at that time to convert the project from a Design and Construct contract to Traditional Design. As a result a new Integrated Consultancy Team was appointed on 8 November 2023, who revisited the RIBA Stage 4 report to update costs and include two additional Special Educational Needs (SEN) classrooms which had received approval since the announcement of the project.  It is anticipated that the revised Stage 4 report will be submitted to my Department for review in July 2024. Due to the increase in both project scope and costs, a business case addendum will be prepared for Department of Finance approval. Ballymena Academy and Cambridge House Grammar School were two of twenty-eight schools announced to advance in planning in March 2022. All twenty-eight projects were paused in April 2023, due to the capital budget position. In my Statement to the Assembly on 13 February I advised that I had taken the decision to re-start seven of the twenty-eight projects, unfortunately Ballymena Academy and Cambridge House Grammar School were not included in the seven projects to progress at this time.
